Through the Super Mario series of video games, it is integral for Mario, Luigi, and their friends to utilize the right power-ups to allow them to defeat the wide variety of enemies they encounter. From the Super Mushroom that allows them to grow and survive attacks to the Fire Flower that provides the ability to throw fireballs at enemies and the Super Star that makes the brothers invincible, one of the keys to defeating Bowser and his army is finding these power-ups and leveraging the abilities they contain at the right time.

Similar to how the power-ups offer unique opportunities through the games, it is important for leaders to take advantage of unique opportunities to advance their business or organization. These power-ups might be hiring the right people, a creative social media campaign, taking suggestions from customers, leveraging technology, or partnering with another organization to plan a mutually beneficial event. Successful leaders take advantage of the right strategies or opportunities to “power-up” their organization, grow more quickly, and help their team reach their goals.

Be thoughtful, patient, and efficient in navigating increasingly difficult challenges

When facing the threat of Bowser throughout the Mushroom Kingdom, it is important not to rush, otherwise you may make unnecessary mistakes. Observing the environment, identifying approaching enemies, and strategizing with partners, like Luigi, on how to navigate obstacles is key to advancing through the levels and worlds. Going too quickly can lead to unnecessary errors and even losing one of Mario or Luigi’s lives by hitting an enemy or falling into a pit, but going too slowly can lead to running out of time and losing the level. Taking a breath, finding the right path through each challenge, relying on trusted partners, and moving efficiently is integral to saving Peach.

When starting or advancing a business or organization, enthusiasm for success can lead to overexcited team members rushing and making mistakes. Observing the larger market to better navigate the industry versus diving in without looking can avoid unnecessary mistakes that might delay or derail momentum toward success. However, leaders also want to avoid moving too slow and being left behind, it is important to find balance between taking advantage of opportunities while mindfully navigating challenges. A mixture of patience and preparedness, and utilizing trusted partnerships, allows organizations to move forward without overlooking opportunities or potential pitfalls.

Learn from mistakes, both yours and others

Any video game player knows that beating a game does not take perfection, it takes trial and error and learning from pitfalls and mistakes. Learning can also occur by watching others or reading guide books, learning what others have done right and what mistakes they have made on each level. Super Mario Bros. begins with the tutorial level where new players learn the basic controls in a lower risk environment before moving forward to increasingly challenging levels. These harder levels have tougher enemies and obstacles, which will likely lead to mistakes, but with extra lives and the ability to reset the game, players can learn from previous mistakes, offering an opportunity to move further through the game.

Organizations face constant pitfalls, so errors are bound to happen. Leaders need to give their team members the room to learn from mistakes and grow. This will help prevent bigger issues later while giving your team the room to take calculated risks that can lead to greater success in the future. When navigating a market or industry, it is also important to observe and research the available case studies or past experiences to avoid repeating mistakes made by others in the field. It s also important to leverage knowledge of how others found success, so you do not have to unnecessarily start from scratch.

item.title: Agent Workflows Are The New Career Currency, And Smart Money Is Paying Attention
item.description: Box CEO Aaron Levie says reimagining enterprise workflows for AI agents is one of the most valuable skills in the economy right now. Venture capital is starting to agree.
item.dc:creator: Josipa Majic Predin, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
The Opportunity Most People Are Missing

In a post on X that circulated widely in enterprise circles, Box co-founder and CEO Aaron Levie laid out a blunt assessment of where value is being created in the current AI transition. "There is a huge opportunity for resourceful and entrepreneurial talent within organizations to go in and reimagine workflows for a world of agents," he wrote.

The post went further than most executive commentary on AI. Levie described a present-tense skills gap inside enterprises, one that early-career professionals are particularly well-positioned to fill.

His argument rests on a structural reality of how AI agents differ from coding assistants. In software development, agents like GitHub Copilot or Claude Code work well out of the box because the context is technical, the users are technical, and the agent already understands the domain. The same shortcut does not exist for the rest of knowledge work.

"For the rest of knowledge work there's no way around this," Levie wrote. "There's really no way to shortcut any of this work. It has to be done by a person or people on the team."

What The Work Actually Involves

Levie broke down the real labor behind agentic workflow deployment: structuring unstructured data so agents can access it, mapping existing processes, building skills and plans for agents to execute, connecting disparate systems, and redesigning the process itself to fit the agent's requirements. Then comes the human layer: deciding where oversight is needed, how outputs get validated, and how exception handling works.

That description maps closely to what Levie has been building at Box. In September 2025, the company launched Box Automate, described by TechCrunch as an operating system for AI agents that breaks workflows into segments, each of which can be augmented with AI or kept deterministic based on risk tolerance. The design explicitly addresses what Levie called the need for "demarcation points" where agents operate within bounded, auditable scope.

"The vast majority of enterprise workflows revolve around unstructured data, which actually represents about 90% of our corporate information," Levie said in a post-earnings call cited by Diginomica. Legal review, M&A due diligence, contract management, clinical research. These are workflows that have been manual precisely because software has never been able to read a document and make a decision. AI agents change that, but only if someone first does the integration work.

The Career Play Hidden In Plain Sight

"You will see a huge growth of roles within enterprises, and people that specialize in this will be hugely valuable in the economy," he wrote. "Great way for early career folks to make a huge dent quickly as well."

Entrepreneur and media founder Alex Lieberman, known on X as @businessbarista, pushed the same thesis in a widely reshared post: "Contrarian take: there's never been a better time for early career professionals to command the attention of leaders in their company. Become the AI Guru in your company."

Lieberman's examples were function-specific. An SDR who maps the inbound process end-to-end and builds an agent to automate it. An engineer who leads internal enablement on Claude Code. A growth marketer who builds a paid media pipeline that mines Reddit for creative ideas, spins up ad variants at scale, and tests spend far more efficiently than any manual process. In each case, the person doing the work does not need to be a senior employee. They need to understand the workflow and know enough to connect it to the tooling.

"You could be the most junior person in your company, but if you're truly viewed as the AI Guru, you are wildly valuable and have way more leverage than you think," Lieberman wrote.

Where The Venture Capital Is Going

Investors are pricing this thesis into their allocations. Agentic AI startups raised $2.8 billion in H1 2025, led by autonomous workplace agents. In Q1 2025, AI captured 53% of all global venture funding, according to Crunchbase data, amounting to $59.6 billion.

The capital is concentrating around platforms with demonstrated enterprise adoption. Glean raised $150 million at a $7.2 billion valuation to expand enterprise AI agents that surface organizational knowledge across tools like Slack and Gmail. Cohere closed $500 million to scale agentic AI tailored for enterprise deployments behind corporate firewalls.

Sovereign wealth funds are amplifying the trend. In the first nine months of 2025, they were involved in AI venture transactions totalling $46 billion, according to EY analysis, with growing interest specifically in agentic AI platforms for workflow automation.

The investor framing has also shifted. According to TechCrunch's survey of active venture partners, the question VCs now ask is whether a product closes the loop from input to execution, not whether it makes recommendations. Jake Flomenberg of Wing Venture Capital put it plainly: "The companies growing fastest are the ones that identified a workflow or security gap created by GenAI adoption, then executed relentlessly on product-market fit."

The Platform Shift Argument

Levie has been making the platform shift argument consistently since at least mid-2025. Speaking at TechCrunch Disrupt in October 2025, he said Box's platforms reach roughly two-thirds of the Fortune 500. From that vantage point, he argued that the current moment is structurally similar to prior platform shifts. "We are in this window right now that we have not been in for about 15 years, which is a complete platform shift happening in tech that's opening up a spot for a new set of companies to emerge."

He is also explicit about scale. At the Axios AI+ Summit in December 2025, Levie said he expects 100 to 1,000 times more agents than people operating inside enterprise software systems. That ratio upends the per-seat pricing model that has underwritten SaaS for two decades, and it creates a new category of human work: the person who designs, validates, and governs the agent layer.

"We are on day one of agent adoption in the enterprise," Levie told GeekWire in May 2025, describing 2025 as a year of pilots and early production deployments. What comes after pilots, if the VC thesis holds, is rapid scaling. The talent that knows how to build what agents need will be scarce before it becomes abundant.

item.title: Riz Ahmed Refuses To Pick A Lane. ‘Bait’ Is Exhibit A
item.description: Oscar winner Riz Ahmed chats to Hannah Abraham about why 'Bait' — his most personal project yet — is meant to keep you guessing.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Abraham, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
“I’m here, I’m present”. A tired Riz Ahmed plops into my Zoom frame, kneading his face in his hands. Just the night before, he’s attended the premiere of his new show Bait. Now, amid rave reviews, he’s knee deep into back-to-back press junkets.

I begin by admitting that I binged Bait all in one sitting. “At no point could I tell where the show was going,” I tell him. Suddenly, the exhaustion flees his face in slow motion as he punches the air in triumph. “YES!”

This interview contains spoilers for Bait.

Riz Ahmed’s Bait Is Impossible to Categorise — On Purpose

The show, which Ahmed created, wrote, and stars in, follows Shah Latif, a struggling British Pakistani actor who fumbles a James Bond audition, and then accidentally-on-purpose ends up in the tabloids as the frontrunner for the role.

What begins as a comedy of mounting humiliations becomes something considerably stranger and more layered: a paranoid thriller, a family drama, a meditation on what it costs to perform a version of yourself palatable enough for the world to accept.

It careens across genres so deliberately that early reactions couldn't decide what it was. Ahmed takes that as a compliment.

"You gotta keep people guessing," he says. "Audiences are so intelligent now. You just got to keep people guessing."

The show arrives into a franchise landscape that has shifted considerably since Bait was first conceived. In March 2025, Amazon MGM Studios formally closed its joint venture deal with veteran Bond producers Barbara Broccoli and Michael G. Wilson, assuming creative control of 007 after six decades of Broccoli family stewardship.

Ahmed developed Bait right around this period of uncertainty about what Bond was, who owned him, and what he was allowed to mean, and he needed Broccoli's permission to use the IP at a moment when her grip on it was loosening.

By his account, everyone around him believed she would never grant it. He asked anyway, making the case that this isn’t really a Bond show at all, that Bond functions here as a symbol of aspiration and a particular flavour of Western male desirability, neither of which have anything to do with Shah and everything to do with what Shah has been told he should want.

She gave her blessing (on the condition that she was never mentioned in the show), and the deal was done over one lunch.

The personal stakes, though, took considerably longer to negotiate — mostly with himself.

How Riz Ahmed Turned Years of Personal Notes Into a James Bond Story

Ahmed has been filling a notepad for the better part of a decade, not with plot points exactly, but with observations about a particular feeling: that life can feel like a continuous audition, the gap between the self you perform in public and the self you actually are, and the specific, cumulative toll of maintaining that gap for a brown man in Britain, where the stakes of being perceived correctly feel higher and the margin for error feels narrower.

"Constantly having to perform this public version of ourselves," he says, “and it’s usually at odds with who we really are or how we really feel.”

This is territory the Oscar-winner has mapped in other forms — his 2016 essay "Typecast as a Terrorist," published in The Guardian as an extract from The Good Immigrant essay anthology, drew on the same bruising intersection of audition rooms and airport interrogation rooms — but Bait goes somewhere more uncomfortably interior.

"Being brown in the West can feel like you're in a spy thriller," he says. "You're already in a spy thriller. What are you auditioning for? You have it whether you like it or not."

“And more often than not, we don’t like it.”

I tell him I also make videos about the war — the one dominating every headlines app on every phone right now, in all its daily, grinding horror — and that the first five minutes made me belly laugh in a way I didn’t fully realize I needed until it escaped me.

Ahmed nods emphatically. “We all need to laugh right now,” he says. “We need to rediscover joy.”

“Laughter bypasses your brain and your biases and all these binaries that we’re trapped in. It can be so healing.”

Ahmed has very outspoken about global conflict himself, calling Israel’s bombing campaign in Gaza 'morally indefensible war crimes’ in 2023 and signing the Film Workers for Palestine boycott pledge in 2025. So when he talks about needing to rediscover joy, I know he isn't speaking in abstractions.

“If there’s one thing that people take from this show," he continues, “let it be those laughs.”

Riz Ahmed on Embarrassing Auditions, Defying Genre, and Convincing Himself

The show’s insistence on defying category (comedy! thriller! family drama! deceased talking animals!) was apparently not a straightforward sell at various stages of development, and Ahmed describes a process of constant persuasion, not just of financiers and collaborators but of himself.

Ahmed wanted Bait in cinemas at the same time as his take on Hamlet, both things existing simultaneously, on purpose, as a statement about what he is and what he refuses to be pinned down as.

"You always have to convince people," he says. "Even convince yourself you know what you're doing when you don't."

What he also knows intimately is the unglamorous texture of auditioning. “Every audition is embarassing,” he laughs.

He describes sending fourteen tapes to a Star Wars director after being given an email address “I probably should not have been given”, and accidentally tearing a hole in Danny Boyle’s shirt during a rather miscalculated audition for Slumdog Millionaire.

For most others, he says, they were so bad he still can't bring himself to follow up.

"Auditioning is by very nature uncomfortable because it makes you self-conscious," he says, “and the best work, the greatest flow that we feel in any kind of life, is the opposite of that.”

Riz Ahmed on the Bait Cast: Guz Khan, Rafe Spall, and a Reality Show Nobody Knew About

The rather excellent cast of Bait (put together by the indomitable Shaheen Baig) is a loving, Adam-Sandler-esque assembly of people Ahmed has wanted to be in a room with again.

Ahmed met Guz Khan, who plays his cousin Zulfi, nearly 20 years ago at Coventry University, where Khan was a student and Ahmed showed up asking him to come support a performance at the student union. Their "second first meeting" came years later when Khan went viral for a tweet about Jurassic Park and DM’d Ahmed on Twitter asking for career guidance, including whether fame required participating in any Illuminati initiations.

Sheeba Chaddha, who also stars across Ahmed in Hamlet, plays the family matriarch Tahira, and Ritu Arya is Yasmin, an ex who writes a provocative article questioning whether James Bond is even worthy of a Muslim man. Patrick Stewart voices a pig’s head. I will not be explaining further.

And then there is Rafe Spall, whose connection to Ahmed goes back to 2006, when the two of them — alongside Tom Hardy, in a pairing that becomes more cosmically implausible the longer you think about it — appeared together on a reality show called The Play's the Thing, in which writers workshopped stage plays for the West End and actors were brought in to help. He mentions this and I make a noise that is not entirely dignified.

"In that reality show," he adds, grinning, "Rafe played an airport security agent."

His life away from set is family-oriented and, by his own description, low-key, so projects like this are among the few occasions he gets to spend sustained time with people he loves, he tells me. "I believe that comedy is about chemistry," he says, "and you bring out the best of yourself when you’re around people you feel you can trust."

The Real Events Behind Bait: What Riz Ahmed Drew From His Own Life

The degree to which Ahmed has chosen to be genuinely exposed in Bait is the most striking thing about both the show and this conversation.

For you see, a large proportion of the details woven into Shah's story are not metaphorical approximations; they are, in several cases, the actual events.

He describes a panic attack he had at Kentish Town Forum, on the same stage where his character has a panic attack in the show, bursting out of that same side exit into that same alley. A memory of being attacked in a park behind his parents' house in Wembley becomes his character's memory too, filmed in that exact staircase, on that exact split of ground.

And then, more surprisingly: the security services. As he’s become more prominent, Ahmed tells me British agencies have reached out to him multiple times over the last several years, asking to meet, asking if he would work with them, asking, in so many words, whether he would spy for them. It happened to him, and so it happens to Shah.

Even the show’s setting across various London neighbourhoods — from popular landmarks like South Kensington to slightly more local-specific areas like Kentish Town — was very intentional.

"I wanted to take our day-to-day experience," he says, "and elevate it to the epic grandeur and importance of what you see in spy thrillers." The chase sequences feel like Bond but unfold across neighbourhood borders that carry specific, loaded meaning for the people crossing them, and the paranoia doesn’t require a single identifiable villain because the system doing the surveilling has already been built into the architecture of daily life.

He invokes Jordan Peele's framing of Get Out — that being Black in America can feel like being in a horror movie, which is why the horror movie got made — and the parallel he draws to Bait is precise: the genre isn't a metaphor for the experience, he's suggesting; the experience is what the genre was always, somewhat imperfectly, describing.

MORE FROM FORBESAmazon’s New James Bond Show Has A 100% On Rotten Tomatoes, With A CatchBy Paul Tassi

He also tells me about a watch given to him as a prize for one of his early films, which he Googled upon receiving and discovered was worth £2500, against the roughly £800 he had made on the production itself.

"For years I didn’t dare take it out of the box, because I thought one day, I’ll have to sell this" he says. "I’m in an unstable profession. I’ve got my dad’s voice in my head."

He takes a beat. "The first time I wore the watch that I won in 2007 or 2008, was last year. Only after I made the show."

There is something almost unbearably human about that image, the watch in the box, the decade and a half of telling himself he hadn't yet earned the right to wear it, the particular psychic weight of growing up in a household where self-employment means uncertainty means you hold onto things because one day you might need to sell them. It is also, in its own way, the whole show — the thing you've been given that you're too afraid to claim.

"As actors, we’re very encouraged to create mystique and try to be a blank canvas, so I used to think acting was about putting on a mask," he says. “Now I think it’s about taking it off.”

“I think it’s about being specific enough about your truth that anyone can find themselves in it.”

'Bait’, created, written by and starring Riz Ahmed, is streaming now on Prime Video.

item.title: Plants On Remote Islands Are Healthier — But Not As Much As Scientists Predicted
item.description: Plants on remote islands get less disease — but isolation reshapes which species dominate, and those species are the most vulnerable to infection.
item.dc:creator: John Drake, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Plants on remote tropical islands get sick less often than plants on islands closer to the mainland. A research team that surveyed 21 islands in the South China Sea found that the farther an island sits from shore, the lower the rates of plant disease. The obvious explanation — distance keeps pests away — turns out to be only half right. The other half of the story is stranger and more interesting: isolation quietly reshapes which plants dominate these islands, and those changes push disease risk back up. The plants are healthier on balance, but the reason involves two forces working against each other.

Hao Qin and colleagues from Hainan University and Lanzhou University reported these findings in a new study in Ecology Letters. Their islands range from tiny — about the size of a city block — to roughly 400 hectares, spread across more than 300 kilometers of open water south of Hainan Island, China. The team set up 89 survey plots across the island chain and recorded 361 tree and shrub species. For each species, they collected leaves and examined them for visible signs of trouble: discoloration, necrosis, wilting and deformities caused by fungi, bacteria and viruses (disease), and tissue loss from insect feeding: chewing, boring, gall formation (herbivory). They confirmed infections under a microscope and scored damage severity on a six-point scale.

Distance Is Everything, Size Is Nothing

The foundational theory of island ecology, known as the theory of island biogeography, was developed by Robert MacArthur and E.O. Wilson in the 1960s. It makes two predictions about biodiversity on islands. Bigger islands hold more species, because in the balance between colonization and extinction species are more likely to persist on large islands than small islands. And more remote islands hold fewer species, because organisms have a harder time getting there. In practice, island size almost always turns out to be the dominant factor. Study after study finds a strong relationship between area and species richness. Distance matters too, but it tends to be the weaker signal.

This study flips that pattern on its head. When it comes to plant disease and herbivory, island size had no direct or indirect effect at the community level. Bigger islands didn’t have sicker plants, despite offering more habitat, more host species and more complex food webs. Distance from the mainland, by contrast, drove everything. It was the only geographic variable that mattered, and it mattered in two different ways that pull in opposite directions.

How Distance Protects

The first effect is intuitive. Fungal infections and other plant diseases were markedly less common on remote islands. Fewer plants showed symptoms at all. The distinction matters: isolation reduced how many plants got sick, not how sick they got. When disease did take hold on a remote island, it was just as severe as on islands closer to shore. Distance is a barrier to arrival, not a treatment.

The ocean is a physical barrier. Most plant pathogens travel passively, carried on wind, water or debris, and open ocean is hard to cross. The farther the island, the fewer disease-causing organisms manage to colonize it. Fewer arrivals means less infection.

Insect damage told a different story. Distance alone didn’t reduce herbivory. The authors speculate that the difference comes down to how organisms move: herbivores disperse actively, while pathogens are passive. A moth can fly. A fungal spore must float on air or water currents and hope for the best.

How Distance Hurts

Here is where the study gets interesting. Distance doesn’t just block pathogens. It also reshapes which plants dominate the island — and the plants that thrive in isolation turn out to be sitting ducks.

The critical clue is leaf size. Across all 89 plots, the single best predictor of both disease and insect damage was how large-leaved the plant community was on average. Islands dominated by big-leaved species had more disease and more herbivory. And remote islands were dominated by exactly those species.

The explanation isn’t about individual plants growing bigger leaves because they’re on a remote island. It’s about entirely different species replacing each other across the island chain. Ecologists call this species turnover: on islands close to the mainland, the dominant tree and shrub species tend to have relatively small leaves. On remote islands, those species are largely gone, replaced by different species with large leaves. The researchers were able to confirm that nearly all of the variation in average leaf size attributable to isolation was driven by this kind of species replacement rather than by individual plants adjusting their leaf size to local conditions.

Why does isolation favor big-leaved species? Remote tropical islands have steadier, warmer climates than the mainland. They rarely experience frost. On the mainland, species with large leaves are at a disadvantage during cold snaps because they are more vulnerable to freeze damage. Those species get pruned back over time. On remote islands, freed from that pressure, large-leaved species flourish. The climate selects for them.

The problem is that big leaves also make easy targets. They offer more surface area for fungal infection and more real estate for hungry insects, like hanging a bigger sign that says “eat here.” So distance simultaneously shields plants from disease by blocking pathogens at the water’s edge and sets them up for disease by favoring species whose leaves are conspicuous to whatever pests do manage to arrive. The shielding effect wins on net, so remote-island plants are healthier. But the margin is thinner than the classical theory would predict.

Why It Matters

Most research on island biology focuses on counting species: which ones, how many, and why. This study asks something different. The vulnerability of an island plant community to disease turns out to hinge not on how many species are present or how far the island is from the mainland, but on what those species look like — specifically, how large their leaves are. That is a functional trait question, not a diversity question, and the distinction matters.

That finding reaches well beyond tropical islands. Anywhere habitat is fragmented, you get the same basic geometry: patches of nature isolated by farmland, suburbs or inhospitable terrain. A forest remnant surrounded by soybean fields, an urban park hemmed in by concrete, a mountaintop ecosystem cut off by warming valleys. All of these are islands in some sense. If isolation filters plant communities the same way in those settings, then understanding disease risk in fragmented landscapes means paying attention not just to which species are present but to the physical profile of the community that remains. Distance doesn’t just control what arrives. It shapes what the survivors look like — and what that appearance costs them.

media:content.media:credit: Xinhua News Agency via Getty Images

item.title: VCs Rethink Startup Moats As AI Compresses Time To Build
item.description: When software becomes trivially cheap to build, defensibility shifts to what takes years to accumulate — data, networks, permits, capital, and physical infrastructure.
item.dc:creator: Josipa Majic Predin, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
A viral post on X by Robert Scoble this week flagged a widely shared investment thesis from Michael Bloch, a partner at early-stage firm Quiet Capital, arguing that artificial intelligence has permanently reordered what makes a business defensible. The post has accumulated nearly 467,000 views. The core argument is simple: AI compresses the time it takes to do things. It does not compress the time it takes for things to happen.

The distinction matters to investors. Bloch frames defensibility around two categories: things that are hard to do, and things that are hard to get. AI, he argues, is rapidly eliminating the first. Building software, maintaining integrations, and embedding a product deeply into a workflow were once meaningful barriers. They are becoming engineering problems measured in hours, not months. What remains are assets that required elapsed, real-world time to accumulate, and that no improvement in AI capability can replicate faster.

Five Moats That Survive the AI Compression

Bloch identifies five categories. The first is living, compounding proprietary data; not static datasets that were merely expensive to collect, but information generated continuously through operations that are themselves defensible. He cites his portfolio company Orchard Robotics, which mounts cameras to farm equipment to track billions of fruit across millions of trees, season after season. Orchard raised a $22 million Series A in September 2025 led by Quiet Capital and Shine Capital. Bloch, who joined the company's board, said that "farmers are forced to make critical decisions based on imprecise data" and that Orchard is "providing the ground truth this massive industry has desperately needed." A competitor would need to drive the same cameras through the same orchards for years to replicate the dataset.

The second moat is network effects. Bloch uses DoorDash, where he was one of the first 50 employees, as the canonical example: drivers, restaurants, and customers reinforce each other's value in ways that cannot be cloned overnight. He suggests the cold start problem may actually get harder as AI makes it trivial to build competing products, because a hundred well-built alternatives now compete to bootstrap the same network. Whoever holds the liquidity already compounds; everyone else fights for scraps.

Third is regulatory permission. Government timelines, Bloch writes, move at the speed of politics, not technology. Bank charters, FDA approvals, and defense procurement contracts are not compressible by intelligence. He points to defense tech firm Anduril, which requires classified contracts and procurement clearances that no AI acceleration can shortcut. As AI capability increases, the regulatory surface area expands rather than contracts, because the stakes rise.

Fourth is capital at scale. Bloch argues this is the most underweighted factor. The endgame of the current technology cycle is physical: chip fabs cost roughly $20 billion, nuclear plants around $10 billion, satellite constellations billions more. The ability to finance and deploy capital at that scale depends on institutional trust, track record, and relationships that take decades to establish.

Fifth is physical infrastructure: factories, power plants, battery networks, and data centers. Bloch highlights Base Power, which has deployed over 100 megawatt-hours of residential battery capacity across Texas homes and is constructing its own manufacturing facility in Austin. Base raised $1 billion in October 2025 at a $4 billion post-money valuation. The company is projecting $70 million in revenue for 2026, up from roughly $12 million in 2025, according to reporting by StockAnalysis. Every battery unit installed is a physical asset in somebody's yard that no amount of AI can manufacture, transport, and interconnect faster than the laws of physics allow.

Investor Consensus Is Converging on the Same View

Bloch’s framework aligns with a broader shift in how venture capital is orienting. OMERS Ventures, in its 2026 VC outlook, noted that tools like Claude Code and Cursor have made building software "astonishingly fast," pushing investors toward opportunities in spatial AI, robotics, autonomy, energy systems, and infrastructure; categories that "sit at the intersection of software, systems, and the physical world." The firm wrote that moats are "now harder to find (and even harder to maintain)" in pure software.

Venionaire Capital’s North America VC 2026 analysis found that capital is clustering around parts of the stack that are harder to replicate; infrastructure, compute, chips, data tooling, and robotics, and becoming more selective elsewhere. "The closer a business model is to defensible infrastructure and real-world deployment, the easier it becomes to justify large cheques," it noted.

Lightspeed Venture Partners, which participated in Base Power’s Series C, cited forecasts that national power demand from AI alone will grow by over 100 gigawatts in the next five years and that Texas peak summer demand is projected to grow by 51 gigawatts, the equivalent of 51 nuclear reactors. The firm described distributed energy storage as an "essential component of the solution" and Base as "a category-defining company." The investment thesis rests on exactly the physical-asset logic Bloch articulates: interconnection queues are years long and only growing longer, and no software shortcut exists.

What Drops Off the List

Bloch is direct about what no longer qualifies. Workflow embeddedness sounds durable until the switching cost is recognized as engineering time — a quantity that is collapsing. Ecosystem lock-in sounds like a fortress until AI can rebuild integrations as fast as they can be described. Software scale, which spreads engineering costs across millions of users, stops mattering when engineering costs approach zero. These were moats against the scarcity of intelligence. That scarcity is ending.

The Percolator newsletter, writing in January 2026, described this dynamic as "differentiation entropy": the natural tendency for model-driven differentiation to diffuse across the ecosystem over time. As models converge in quality and cost, value shifts from the model layer toward less visible but more durable structures. The analysis concluded that defensibility now depends less on invention and more on execution velocity and integration depth — a framing consistent with Bloch's.

The Meta-Moat

What unites all five categories is a shared property: they required years of real-world elapsed time to accumulate. Network density takes years of human adoption. Regulatory approval takes years of political process. Infrastructure takes years to build. Proprietary data takes years to compound. Capital relationships take decades to earn. Bloch calls time that cannot be parallelized "the meta-moat underneath all five."

For founders and investors, the filter reduces to a single question: is your defensibility bottlenecked by intelligence, or by years? The former is on borrowed time. The latter may be the most durable advantage available in the current era.

Michael Bloch's full essay is available at michaelxbloch.com. Robert Scoble's post amplifying it is at x.com/scobleizer.

item.title: An Entrepreneur’s Mission To Help Patients Breathe More Easily At Home
item.description: Valerie Obenchain frames her company's work around the belief that oxygen-dependent patients deserve the same level of care continuity as hospital patients.
item.dc:creator: Mandeep Rai, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Healing does not end when a patient leaves the hospital, yet in modern healthcare, discharge often marks the point where risk increases. For many patients, this transition introduces uncertainty and anxiety.

This is a gap that respiratory therapist turned entrepreneur Valerie Obenchain – founder of Advanced Interactive Response Systems (AIRS) - has spent much of her career trying to close after witnessing this firsthand with her own grandmother.

Valerie focuses on a deceptively simple idea: patients who depend on oxygen should feel just as safe at home as they do in a hospital.

A Personal Problem Hidden in Plain Sight

Valerie’s journey into healthcare innovation did not begin in a startup accelerator. It began through her own experience as a granddaughter.

“My grandmother developed Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD),” she recalls. “It is a progressive lung disease that makes breathing difficult and requires a person to rely on oxygen. Though my grandmother was active and independent, the oxygen support followed her everywhere. It was really difficult to ascertain whether the equipment was working smoothly until it wasn’t!” That uncertainty became a defining motivation.

Home oxygen therapy is increasingly prescribed to people living with chronic lung conditions such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) or pulmonary vascular diseases.

Yet the period immediately after discharge can be one of the most fragile phases of care. Research shows that post-acute care is still underused, with around 45% of patients opting for post-acute services, despite the fact that complications, medication errors and setbacks are common after leaving hospital.

For oxygen-dependent patients, the risks can be particularly significant. Oxygen therapy is not static as needs fluctuate based on activity, sleep and disease progression, yet once patients return home, monitoring often becomes sporadic.

Valerie Obenchain: From Respiratory Therapist to Founder

Valerie Obenchain began her career collaborating directly with patients as a respiratory therapist, an experience that exposed her daily to the realities of chronic lung disease and oxygen therapy.

“I started my career as a Respiratory Therapist, and I also experienced COPD up close through my grandmother,” she says.

Over time, the pattern became impossible to ignore – “home oxygen users often have limited visibility into whether therapy is working in real life, outside a controlled setting,” she tells me. Patients who seemed stable could deteriorate quickly at home. Families struggled to manage equipment they had never been trained to use. And clinicians often lacked visibility into what was happening once patients left the hospital.

“We spent years in customer discovery with patients, caregivers, and clinicians to understand the failure points,” she explains. “When it became clear that existing tools weren’t built for the realities of home oxygen, we started designing our own.”

Simplifying oxygen therapy at home

That process eventually led to the founding of Advanced Interactive Response Systems (AIRS), a company focused on improving how oxygen therapy is monitored outside hospital walls.

The platform monitors oxygen supply and patient activity, while integrating with a mobile app and clinician portal designed to provide visibility into therapy outside the hospital setting. The company has secured patents internationally and continues to develop additional technologies designed to simplify oxygen therapy at home.

Obenchain’s work has also extended into research collaborations, including partnerships with health systems and facilities for monitoring technologies in improving patient outcomes.

Rethinking the Gap Between Hospital and Homecare

The broader issue AIRS is trying to address extends beyond oxygen therapy alone. It reflects a structural challenge within healthcare: the gap between hospital care and what happens after discharge.

Families often become informal caregivers without medical training. For elderly patients or people with limited mobility, frequent hospital visits can be physically demanding and costly. Yet remaining in hospital can increase exposure to infections and dramatically raise healthcare costs.

“All aspects of healthcare are monitored stringently at a hospital,” Valerie says. “There has been a steady rise in the prescription of oxygen post discharge. The mortality rate among patients requiring oxygen therapy is high during the 60-day follow-up window and a significant proportion of these patients face health challenges.”

Effective homecare sits at the centre of modern healthcare systems trying to balance patient safety with sustainability.

A Values-Driven Mission: Helping People Breathe Easy

Despite the technological focus, Valerie frames the company’s work primarily around values - particularly the belief that oxygen-dependent patients deserve the same level of care continuity as hospital patients.

The core philosophy behind AIRS is not simply about building devices. It is about restoring confidence for patients who must live with chronic respiratory conditions.

“Oxygen needs aren’t static; they shift with activity, sleep, and disease progression, yet the system still treats home oxygen as a ‘set it and forget it’ prescription. AIRS is bringing telemedicine and remote monitoring into supplemental oxygen therapy so clinicians can support accurate titration, and patients and families can have real reassurance. In short, we help people ‘Breathe easy’.”

Her critique of the healthcare system is not directed at individuals but at structural blind spots.

“The biggest system failure is what happens after discharge: we send patients home with a therapy that can be dynamic and high-risk, then largely stop measuring it,” she explains. “Oxygen needs can change with exertion, sleep, infection, or disease progression, yet reassessment is often episodic and reactive.”

“What needs to change is straightforward: treat home oxygen like a monitored therapy, not a commodity,” she says.

The Future of Care Happens at Home

If the next decade of healthcare is defined by anything, it may be the expansion of care into patients’ homes. Remote monitoring, telemedicine and digital health tools are reshaping how chronic conditions are managed outside clinical settings.

For Valerie Obenchain, the goal remains grounded in the human experience. Her grandmother’s experience continues to shape how she thinks about healthcare design: systems should not simply function - they should reassure.

For patients living with respiratory disease, breathing is already hard enough. The healthcare system should not make it harder.

item.title: Bluesky’s Attie Tests Who Really Controls Social AI
item.description: Bluesky's new Attie, a natural-language agent, makes the case that social media does not have to be locked inside a few giant platforms.
item.dc:creator: Ron Schmelzer, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Bluesky has spent years making a case that social media does not have to be locked inside a few giant platforms. Introduced in closed beta at the ATmosphere conference, the company’s new Attie makes the case that your feed and interface to social media should be yours as well.

Attie is a natural-language agent built on the AT Protocol. Its promise is that a user can describe the kind of feed they want, and the system builds it. Ask for more reporting on sustainable practices, or more balanced political commentary or more neighborhood arts coverage, and Attie turns that request into a working feed without having to fiddle with obscure settings.

In a market dominated by black-box recommendation systems, Attie presents an alternative to the core assumption of the modern social web that platforms should decide what matters, and that those platforms should also determine how relevance works and which signals deserve priority in order to put posts or items on your feeds. Bluesky is arguing that AI should reflect the user’s intent and interests, not quietly reshape it for the benefit of the platform.

Feeds Built from Interests And Not Inference

Most major social networks work in roughly the same way. They watch data points, measure engagement and activities and then infer from there based on what will keep you longer and more engaged on the platform. All user’s behavioral activities are factored in such as clicks, hovering or lingering on post, follows, shares, and even scrolling behavior. All of it becomes raw material for a recommendation engine designed to keep attention in motion. That model has created some of the largest technology businesses on earth because it feeds the advertising and subscription-driven business models that reward that sort of engagement.

Still, it leaves users with very little real control. People can mute or follow accounts, maybe tweak a few ranking options, but the underlying logic stays hidden. The system keeps learning from behavior, and the company keeps control over how that behavior gets interpreted, and the algorithm controls what you can see. Attie flips that around, at least in theory.

Instead of forcing the machine to guess what a person wants, it lets the person say it. That turns preference from inferred signals into explicit instructions. That may prove more important than any one feature inside the product. The fight here is not really about feed customization. It is about who gets to define the terms of personalization in the first place.

Why The AT Protocol Changes The Stakes

If Attie were just another AI layer dropped into a closed platform, it would still be interesting. The reason it draws more attention is the foundation underneath it. Bluesky built Attie on the AT Protocol, an open, federated standard for social applications. That matters because open protocols create the possibility that identity, data, and social graphs do not have to remain trapped inside one company’s walls. In principle, a user can carry more of their social presence across services, and developers can build on shared rails instead of asking permission from a gatekeeper.

In that context, Attie is doing more than generating feeds. It is making a larger claim about how AI could fit into an open social stack. For years, personalization has been one of the strongest forms of platform lock-in. The service gets better at predicting what holds your attention and your habits become part of its moat. The recommendations improve, at least enough to keep you from leaving, and the company keeps the full benefit of that accumulated learning. Users rarely get transparency into why the feed looks the way it does, and they almost never get portability.

Attie points toward a different way of getting engagement. If personalization can be described in plain language and built on open infrastructure, then recommendation logic is much more understandable and controllable. That does not automatically solve the problem of lock-in, but it does challenge the idea that personalization has to belong to the platform alone.

The Technical Problem Is Harder Than A Demo

One of the challenges of making this work is that LLMs are not precise and people aren’t always the best at prompt engineering. They ask for contradictory things and leave out context. They change their minds and can use fuzzy, emotional and often inconsistent language to describe their interests.

A system like Attie has to turn those messy requests into feed logic that actually works. Not just once in a demo, but repeatedly for lots of users under unpredictable conditions. Large language models are good at translation, summarization, and routing tasks, but they are less reliable when nuance piles up, edge cases multiply or the request depends on subtle cultural distinctions. A feed request that sounds perfectly reasonable to a user may still produce an experience that feels skewed, brittle or overly literal once the machine executes it.

Likewise, feeds might take advantage of those inconsistencies. Any tool that lets users shape information flows in natural language will run into questions about abuse, safety, ranking and visibility. If the system builds feeds from open-ended instructions, it has to decide what kinds of requests are acceptable, which sources get trusted and how to handle outputs that technically satisfy a prompt while still producing a bad social outcome.

If Attie gains traction, the incumbent platforms might also add more AI-driven feed and customization to their platforms. But they will have a harder time copying the underlying premise. A large ad-driven platform can add a conversational interface to customize feed preferences and even layer more user controls into the product they can market as user empowerment. But that does not mean it will give up the economics of centralized ranking, proprietary models and engagement-led incentives that form the basis for their revenue.

item.title: This $20,000-A-Night Villa Was Abandoned In The Alps For 40 Years
item.description: Forestis restores a 1912 doctor's residence on the Plose massif plank by plank. Architect Armin Sader explains why the villa inverts everything the main hotel does.
item.dc:creator: Paul Jebara, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
In 1912, Habsburg physicians identified a south-facing slope on Northern Italy’s Plose massif as possessing four conditions they considered superior to better-known Alpine health stations for treating tuberculosis: pure artesian spring water filtered through dolomite rock at 6,100 feet; roughly 300 days of annual sunshine on a slope sheltered from northern winds; clean mountain air where Adriatic currents meet Alpine masses; and a mild microclimate produced by all three converging at exactly the right altitude. They commissioned a sanatorium. World War I halted construction. Soon after, the Treaty of Saint-Germain transferred South Tyrol to Italy in 1919. The complex served briefly as a climate station for veterans, then as a retreat for clergymen through the 1950s, before being abandoned entirely. The forest enjoyed four decades reclaiming it.

When South Tyrolean hotelier Alois Hinteregger stumbled on the overgrown site around 2000, noticing a mysterious roofline from a nearby family hut, he climbed a fence into dense vegetation and entered a dim wooden building that had been invisible from every surrounding path for decades. His son Stefan and partner Teresa Unterthiner spent eight years developing what became Forestis, a 62-suite retreat that opened in 2020 with three larch-clad towers rising vertically through the tree canopy, designed by Brixen-based architect Armin Sader of ASAGGIO to avoid felling a single mature spruce. The towers look outward. Every suite faces west toward the jagged Odle range and the solitary Peitlerkofel, the northernmost summit of the Dolomites proper. There is no artwork on the walls. The view is the artwork.

The Forestis Villa, which opened in February 2025 roughly a hundred yards from the main structure, does something different with that inheritance. The five-bedroom, nearly 13,000-square-foot residence occupies the original doctor's house, built in 1912 to serve the physicians of the planned sanatorium. Where the towers are vertical and transparent, the villa is low, enclosed, and partially shielded by old-growth forest. Its traditional Kastenfenster, the double-paned box windows characteristic of Austro-Hungarian Alpine construction, interrupt the Dolomite views rather than frame them. The landing between floors is oddly uneven. The floorboards creak. The carved entrance ornamentation carries the visual grammar of regional Jugendstil. None of this was a problem to solve. All of it was the material Sader chose to protect.

"The design approach for the villa is quite different from that of the main hotel," Sader says. "In this case, the focus was not on framing views, but on preserving and celebrating the character of the historic building itself." Teresa Unterthiner and Stefan Hinteregger obtained the original hand-drawn construction plans from the Vienna city archive and led the restoration in close cooperation with the Provincial Heritage Office in Bolzano. Specialist restorers familiar with early-20th-century Tyrolean craft techniques oversaw the execution. The pinewood facade was taken down, restored, and reassembled plank by plank. Every larch and spruce floorboard was numbered, removed, cleaned, dried, and returned to its original position. Doors bearing antique cast-iron handles typical of Alpine holiday homes went through the same process. The coffered wooden ceilings were preserved. The imposing original staircase was restored in place.

Yet the real intervention happens where you can't see it. A roughly 4,300-square-foot private spa occupies the basement, with a heated indoor-outdoor pool, Finnish sauna, steam room, cold pool, treatment room, and a relaxation lounge with a wood-burning fireplace and a glass panel that opens directly onto the snow. "The spa area, which is a completely new addition, was deliberately placed in the basement," Sader shares. "This allowed us to meet all technical and spatial requirements without affecting the historic character of the villa above." The same logic governed the underfloor heating, electrical systems, and five new en-suite bathrooms. While the infrastructure descends, heritage remains at eye level.

Carpentry Group Frener, based in St. Leonhard just a few miles down the mountain, executed the woodwork under managing director Robert Eisenstecken and master carpenter Albin Frener. Their commission ran from structural beams to hand-carved bowls and spoons, all from solid untreated local timber. Stonemason Manuel Moling contributed bespoke stone finishes using techniques that bridge traditional Tyrolean craft and contemporary application. The material palette follows ASAGGIO's "four elements" framework applied across both the main hotel and the villa, where stone represents water, glass represents air, untreated wood represents climate, and natural linen fabrics woven in a Trentino mill represent sun. Synthetic materials are nowhere to be found in the building.

The furniture tells the clearest story about what separates the two structures architecturally. At the main hotel, Sader's pieces are clean-lined and contemporary, designed to recede against the views. In the villa, every new object was built to look as though it had always been there. "A contemporary language would have felt out of place here," Sader admits. "All new elements, including the furniture, were carefully developed to resonate with the existing fabric, often with a deliberately traditional appearance." A child's cradle has been stripped to bare wood. Roof beams from the original structure were repurposed as a wine-tasting table in the stone cellar. Ceramics by a Brixen-based artist sit alongside preserved architectural details. Meanwhile, the hotel's sole decorative object, a 130-million-year-old fossil, simply has no equivalent here. "The historic building itself, with its materials, craftsmanship, and architectural details, becomes the artwork," Sader says.

The villa accommodates up to ten guests with a dedicated chef, butler, and concierge, and as a first for Forestis, welcomes children under 14. A roughly 10,800-square-foot private garden faces south toward the Dolomites, with a heated pool, herb garden, outdoor kitchen, and a path leading directly to the Plose ski slopes. The forest reclaimed this building once. Sader's achievement is making it feel as though it never left.

Rates start at approximately $20,700 per night.

item.title: 4 High-Value Resume Skills Students Should Develop This Summer
item.description: The strongest student resumes aren't built on titles. Discover 4 high-value skills—from entrepreneurial thinking to adaptability—that students can develop this summer.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Hernholm, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
The World Economic Forum estimates that by 2030, 78 million new jobs will be created worldwide, even as millions of existing positions are being phased out or redefined. Job titles are constantly changing, which makes resume skills built around them increasingly unreliable.

Instead of focusing solely on which internship to pursue, the more important question is: Which valuable skills should you develop, and how can you effectively demonstrate your proficiency in them?

1. Entrepreneurial Thinking Is A Resume Skill That Shows Ownership

Employers consistently rank initiative and ownership among the traits they most struggle to find in early-career candidates. Employers are looking for someone who can identify a gap, take action on it, and see it through without being managed at every step.

Students in WIT (Whatever It Takes) are expected to launch real projects within days of starting the program, rather than participating in simulations. This hands-on approach places teens in control and often encourages them to step outside their comfort zones. It also transforms the typical classroom dynamics, where students usually rely on teachers for direction. At WIT, the emphasis is on experiential learning and overcoming challenges.

A summer project, a freelance client, a neighborhood service—any of these builds the same muscle. What signals ownership on a resume:

  • Identifying a gap and acting on it consistently
  • Creating something from start to finish
  • Making decisions and being accountable for outcomes
  • Completing work that exists beyond an assigned task

2. Leadership Is A Resume Skill That Doesn't Require A Title

The Future of Jobs Report 2025, published by the World Economic Forum, identifies leadership and social influence as among the fastest-growing skill categories across industries.

For high school and college students, gaining leadership experience doesn’t necessarily require a formal title. It means taking the initiative to move a project forward.

Here are some leadership experiences that can enhance a resume:

  • Organizing a team event or community initiative
  • Coordinating peers to meet a shared deadline or goal
  • Presenting results to an audience or panel
  • Managing conflicts and finding resolutions

Consider a teenager who started a dog-walking business and accidentally double-booked two clients for the same afternoon. To resolve the situation, the teenager had to call one of the clients to explain the mistake, reschedule, and maintain a positive relationship. This five-minute conversation required more leadership skills than most classroom projects demand over an entire semester.

3. Communication Is A Resume Skill That Service Jobs Build Fast

A student who spent the summer waiting tables may have developed stronger communication skills than the one who spent it shadowing in a corporate office.

Customer-facing service jobs—restaurants, retail, camps, front desks—require constant, high-stakes communication. According to a 2024 NACE survey, oral communication is one of the top skills employers look for when hiring recent graduates. Service jobs are especially effective at developing communication skills because they offer immediate feedback instead of relying solely on performance reviews. Here are some valuable communication experiences gained from service jobs:

  • Resolving issues with frustrated customers
  • Coordinating with a team in real time while under pressure
  • Clearly explaining information to someone who may be resistant to it
  • Adjusting tone and approach when interacting with different types of individuals

4. Adaptability Is A Resume Skill Built In Unpredictable Environments

The Future of Jobs Report 2025 specifically calls out resilience, flexibility, and the capacity for continuous learning as defining skills for the decade ahead. Adaptability can't be listed on a resume and verified, but it can be demonstrated through the stories a student tells.

The stories shared by students often stem from real situations where things did not go as planned, necessitating a change in approach or resulting in

unexpected outcomes. Students who spend their summers in structured, low-pressure environments usually have less to discuss in interviews. In contrast, those who have dealt with true uncertainty tend to have more engaging stories to share.

Situations that build resume-worthy adaptability:

  • A project that didn't go as planned and required a new approach
  • A team dynamic that shifted and had to be rebuilt
  • Learning a new tool or process with no prior training
  • Staying focused and productive when direction wasn't clear

How To Build A Resume That Shows More Than A Title

The most effective summer strategy for a resume-build isn't a single prestigious internship. It's a combination: a real project with ownership attached, a job that requires active communication and problem-solving, and some exposure to situations that don't come with a manual.

Students don't have to choose between entrepreneurship programs and service jobs. Both produce resume evidence. What changes the outcome is whether the student is paying attention to what they're actually learning—and whether they can articulate it.

The strongest resume skills students can demonstrate are judgment, communication, and adaptability, and those qualities hold their value in a job market increasingly shaped by AI and automation.

item.title: Easter Sees Pet Poisonings Spike. Here’s How To Protect Dogs And Cats
item.description: Dr. Tina Wismer, senior director of ASPCA Poison Control, shares tips for keeping dogs and cats safe from common culprits like chocolate and Easter lilies.
item.dc:creator: Jen Reeder, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
While Easter can bring fun festivities like egg hunts and candy-filled baskets, it can also unfortunately lead to an urgent hop to the emergency room for dogs and cats.

The main culprits for cats are Easter lilies, according to Tina Wismer, veterinarian, board certified veterinary toxicologist and senior director of ASPCA Poison Control. In 2025, calls to the ASPCA Poison Control hotline (888-426-4435; $110 consulting fee) about cats eating lilies spiked 50% on Easter and the days surrounding the holiday.

If a dog eats a lily, it typically leads to mild stomach upset, which isn’t too concerning. But cats and lilies do not mix.

“With cats, it takes such small amounts. A bite of a leaf or a bite of a petal or grooming the pollen off their face is enough to cause kidney failure,” Dr. Wismer said. “It’s horribly, horribly toxic. If you have a cat, no lilies in the household ever, please.”

The biggest Easter danger for dogs is chocolate, which is always a problem. In 2025, the ASPCA Poison Control hotline received over 51,000 calls about pets eating chocolate.

“That works out to an average of 140 calls about that a day,” she said. “That goes up by 10 to 15 percent during and around Easter. It’s usually a couple days before and a few days after.”

While chocolate is toxic to both dogs and cats, dogs tend to get into the most trouble.

“With cats and chocolate, they like to sample it. They’ll take a bite or two, but because cats don’t have sweet taste buds, they don’t eat enough to be a problem — unlike dogs,” she said. “For dogs, of course, the entire pan of brownies is one serving.”

The smaller the dog, the less chocolate they have to eat to show symptoms, which range from vomiting and diarrhea to elevated heart rates, tremors and seizures. Unsweetened baking chocolate and dark chocolate are more concentrated — and more toxic — than milk chocolate by weight.

Keeping Dogs And Cats Safe From Easter Pet Poisonings

Other Easter dangers for pets include sugar-free candy and gum that contain the sugar substitute xylitol, raisins (since grapes are toxic to dogs and cats), and even the plastic grass in Easter baskets, according to Dr. Wismer.

“We also see an issue with the plastic Easter grass that can actually cause an obstruction in animals in their intestinal tract. And because it’s kind of sharp and skinny, it sometimes can cut through the intestinal tract,” she said. “So use shredded paper or something that’s a little more environmentally friendly.”

To help keep pets safe on and around Easter, Dr. Wismer recommends that if Easter baskets are set out overnight, make sure pets are behind a closed door or baby gate so they don’t have access to the baskets.

She stressed the importance of warning children and guests about keeping Easter lilies, chocolate and other toxins away from dogs and cats.

“It’s training your children and training your family that we need to be careful with these items around our pets,” she said. “Just because it’s good for you doesn’t necessarily mean it’s good for them, too.”

If you’re hosting guests for Easter dinner, discourage them from feeding pets from the table to avoid stomach upset or pancreatitis. Additionally, keep purses off the ground and out of reach of dogs and cats since they might contain pills, food, sugar-free gum, Ibuprofen or Tylenol, Dr. Wismer advised.

“Grandma’s purse is a very dangerous place if you’re a dog,” she said.

If a pet is showing symptoms or you suspect they’ve eaten chocolate, lilies or another toxic treat, don’t hesitate to seek veterinary help.

“Always call your veterinarian first and follow their advice,” Dr. Wismer said. “If they’re not open, you can certainly give your local emergency clinic or us a call.”

Hopefully, by taking precautions, everyone in the family can enjoy a fun and safe Easter.

“It’s all about prevention,” Dr. Wismer said. “You want to enjoy time with your family, not spend it at the emergency clinic.”

item.title: Marc Andreessen's 'Casual Ownership' Philosophy Puts Silicon Valley's Anxiety Culture On Trial
item.description: Marc Andreessen, co-founder and general partner at Andreessen Horowitz, which manages over $90 billion in assets under management, used a mid-March 2026 podcast appearance to endorse a YouTube creator's self-improvement philosophy — one built around a deliberately provocative name and a surprisingly simple premise: stop internalizing outcomes and move on.
item.dc:creator: Josipa Majic Predin, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Marc Andreessen, co-founder and general partner at Andreessen Horowitz, which manages over $90 billion in assets under management, used a mid-March 2026 podcast appearance to endorse a YouTube creator’s self-improvement philosophy, one built around a deliberately provocative name and a surprisingly simple premise: stop internalizing outcomes and move on.

The concept is called retardmaxxing.

Note: This article references a term that is widely considered offensive and has historically been used as a slur. It is included solely because it is the exact language used by the individuals discussed and is necessary to accurately report on the cultural phenomenon.

Andreessen encountered it through a friend and traced it to Elisha Long, a U.S.-based YouTuber with 251,000 subscribers and over 26 million total views across a channel devoted almost entirely to the philosophy. "He's like my new life coach," Andreessen told host Harry Stebbings on the 20VC podcast. "It's basically just like: go to work, do a good job, come home. It's fine."

On March 17, 2026, Andreessen posted on X: "Current status: Retardmaxxing." The following day, he followed up: "Status: Day 19,977 of retardmaxxing. Things going really well."

Casual Ownership: The Quieter Alternative to a Decade of Extreme Accountability

The substantive claim Andreessen made, and the one most likely to be lost in the noise around the meme’s name, is a direct critique of extreme ownership, the accountability framework popularized by former Navy SEAL Jocko Willink. Extreme ownership holds that leaders must accept total personal responsibility for every outcome, failure included. It became a fixture of founder and executive culture through the 2010s, particularly in venture-backed startups where post-mortems, blame architecture, and relentless self-interrogation were normalized as markers of operational seriousness.

"It's the simpler form of extreme ownership," Andreessen said. "It's the form that says I don't need to take all this in on myself. I can just let it go."

The distinction matters. Extreme ownership was designed for military operations, where accountability cascades are structurally necessary — failures repeat if no one owns them. Applied wholesale to startup culture, it produced something different: a generation of founders trained to convert every setback into personal liability, generating psychic overhead without proportionate improvement in outcomes.

Retardmaxxing, as Long frames it and Andreessen describes it, inverts the weight. The situation happened. It is what it is. You move forward. Start a company — it succeeds or fails, it's fine. Ask someone out and get turned down, it's fine. Have too much to eat one night, it's fine. The throughline is disengagement from rumination, not disengagement from effort.

The YouTuber Behind the Framework

Long's channel, @ElishaLong, has published over 1,600 videos with titles including "I started Retardmaxxing and life got better," "God blesses his most retarded warriors," and "Ride The Tiger Retardmaxxing." The Retardmaxx Podcast is available on Apple Podcasts and Spotify. According to Urban Dictionary, the term Long popularized is defined as "the philosophy of taking action without overthinking things" — the idea being that overthinking, not poor judgment, is the primary obstacle to performance.

Long's own framing, per his channel's content, extends the concept into relationships, diet, training, and career: act on instinct, cut what isn't working, stop optimizing and start executing. The philosophical parallel to Stoicism is direct. Epictetus's foundational teaching distinguishes between what is within our control and what is not, and holds that distress arises from conflating the two. Retardmaxxing collapses that framework into a single, meme-compatible response.

On X, Long has posted: "Keep Retardmaxxing and don't you dare go hollow."

Why It Reached Andreessen — and Why VCs Are Paying Attention

Andreessen's cultural pronouncements carry weight that extends beyond personal branding. a16z raised $15 billion in January 2026, its largest fundraise on record, capturing an estimated 18% of all U.S. venture capital raised in 2025 according to Crunchbase — this in what the National Venture Capital Association called the slowest fundraising year for VC firms since 2017. In the same month, Andreessen was named to President Trump's Presidential Council of Advisors on Science and Technology (PCAST), co-chaired by David Sacks.

The practical relevance for the VC world is straightforward. Fund managers making hundreds of bets annually cannot afford extended post-loss spirals. The question is whether deliberate non-rumination is compatible with the pattern-recognition discipline that underpins investment judgment — or whether it is a rationalization for avoiding accountability altogether. Andreessen's implicit argument is that the two are separable: you can release the emotional weight of a bad outcome without erasing the analytical lesson.

The 20VC episode also included Andreessen's now-widely-circulated claim that he practices introspection 'as little as possible' or 'zero,' and that great historical figures did not engage in self-examination. Reporting from Let's Data Science noted the clip drew criticism from Paul Graham and Seth Bannon, while Elon Musk expressed agreement. Retardmaxxing and the anti-introspection argument are adjacent but distinct: one is about emotional release, the other about skipping self-analysis entirely. Andreessen appears to endorse both.

The Name Is the Problem — and the Point

The term itself uses a clinical word for intellectual disability that is widely considered a slur. That is not incidental to its spread: the deliberate provocation is part of the branding. Republic World noted that the term "uses deliberately provocative language to describe the opposite of making an optimal decision," positioning the shock value as a feature that signals rejection of conventional self-improvement culture. Commentary on X from Peer Richelsen observed that "the normalization of the word retard needs to be studied," framing the linguistic shift as a cultural phenomenon worth tracking.

The name also functions as a filter. By attaching a genuine idea to language that mainstream media and corporate culture cannot repeat without controversy, the concept self-selects for an audience that has already opted out of performative seriousness — which is, of course, the audience most likely to already be practicing what it describes.

Whether the underlying framework earns a cleaner label — casual ownership, situational release, low-friction resilience — remains an open question. For now, the person managing more venture capital than any other individual on the planet has declared it his current operating status, and pointed his 1.2 million X followers to a 251,000-subscriber YouTube channel in the process.

item.title: UFC 327 Main Card Set With Two Title Fights
item.description: The UFC has announced the official main card for UFC 327. The April 11 event two title fights at the top of the marquee.
item.dc:creator: Trent Reinsmith, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Barring a draw, the UFC will crown a new light heavyweight champion on Saturday, April 11, when the fight promotion makes its annual stop at Kaseya Center in Miami. Florida. In addition to that title fight, the flyweight belt will be on the line in the co-main event of UFC 327.

With Alex Pereira abandoning the UFC light heavyweight belt to pursue a title in a third UFC weight division — he faces Ciryl Gane for the interim heavyweight strap at the UFC White House card — former 205-pound titleholder Jiri Prochazka battles Carlos Ulberg for the vacant 205-pound title in the headliner of UFC 327. In the UFC 327 co-main event, UFC 125-pound titleholder Joshua Van puts his title on the line for the first time. Van faces Tatsuro Taira in that scrap.

UFC 327 streams on Paramount+.

UFC 327 Main Card

Jiri Prochazka vs. Carlos Ulberg
Joshua Van vs. Tatsuro Taira
Curtis Blaydes vs. Josh Hokit
Dominick Reyes vs. Johnny Walker
Azamat Murzakanov vs. Paulo Costa

UFC 327 Main Event: Jiri Prochazka vs. Carlos Ulberg

Jiri Prochazka (32-5-1) entered the UFC in 2020 with a 26-3-1 record. At the time, he was coming off a knockout win over former UFC fighter C.B. Dollaway, which earned him the Rizin light heavyweight title.

Prochazka was riding a 10-fight winning streak ahead of his UFC debut, with nine knockout wins.

The former Rizin champ announced himself to UFC fans with a second-round knockout of former UFC light heavyweight title challenger Volkan Oezdemir at UFC 251. The UFC awarded Prochazka a “Performance of the Night” bonus for that knockout. He followed that stoppage with another knockout, decking another ex-UFC title challenger, Dominick Reyes, in the second round of their UFC on ESPN main event matchup.

The UFC, sensing it had something special in Prochazka, then booked him for a title shot. That fight came in June 2022 against reigning champ Glover Teixeira. That fight was a spirited affair, but Prochazka once again scored a stoppage victory when he picked up his first submission in more than nine years, winning the scrap in the fifth round via rear-naked choke.

Prochazka’s title reign was short-lived. A nasty shoulder injury forced him to withdraw from a scheduled rematch against Teixeira, and he vacated the title at that time.

Prochazka made his return to the UFC Octagon in November 2023 against former UFC middleweight champ Alex Pereira. Their UFC 295 headlining bout was for the vacant light heavyweight crown. Pereira claimed the belt with a second-round knockout win, handing Prochazka his first loss since ex-Strikeforce light heavyweight kingpin Muhammed Lawal decked him in late 2015.

Prochazka bounced back from that defeat with a “Performance of the Night” bonus-winning knockout of Aleksandar Rakic at UFC 300.

That win earned Prochazka another shot at Pereira and his title. That bout headlined UFC 303 in June 2024. Pereira retained his belt with a second-round knockout win.

Prochazka followed that setback with back-to-back third-round knockout wins. Stopping Jamahal Hill at UFC 311, and Khalil Rountree at UFC 320. He earned “Performance of the Night” bonuses for both stoppages and added a “Fight of the Night” bonus for the Rountree matchup. Prochazka is the No. 2 ranked fighter in the official UFC light heavyweight rankings.

Carlos Ulberg (13-1) made his UFC debut in March 2021 after winning a contract on a Dana White’s Contender Series card. Ulberg’s knockout win on the DWCS moved his record to 5-0. At the time Ulberg was known mostly as a member of the City Kickboxing team out of Auckland, New Zealand. Today, he is Carlos Ulberg, the N0. 6 ranked fighter in the UFC’s light heavyweight division.

Ulberg’s official UFC run began at UFC 259 with a loss. Kennedy Nzechukwu knocked out Ulberg in a Fight of the Night bonus-winning scrap on that night in March 2021.

Since that defeat, Ulberg has gone 9-0 with six stoppage victories. The 35-year-old has earned five UFC knockouts and one submission, while racking up four fight-night bonuses.

In his three most recent fights, Ulberg has wins over Volkan Oezdemir, Jan Blachowicz and Dominick Reyes. Oezdemir and Reyes are former UFC title challengers, while Blachowicz is an ex-champ. Ulberg’s most recent win was his Fight Night main event first-round knockout of Reyes in September 2025. The Reyes stoppage came after back-to-back three-round decision wins. Ulberg is the No. 3 ranked UFC 205-pounder.

We will have more on the UFC 327 fight card as fight night approaches.

item.title: The Thought Doer’s Proof
item.description: A leader tests AI as an operating partner, turning 400 hours into $12M of output—and revealing why the future belongs to those who build, not just advise.
item.dc:creator: John Winsor, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
A few weeks ago, I wrote in the Harvard Business Review that thought leadership is dying. That we’re drowning in polished, AI-generated insight that sounds authoritative but wasn’t forged through experience.
