I argued that organizations should stop hiring people who describe the future and start hiring people who build it. I called that shift thought doership.

The piece struck a nerve. The responses that flooded my inbox fell into two categories. The first was recognition—leaders who’d spent six figures on transformation keynotes and had nothing to show for it. The second was a question I should have anticipated: OK, but what does thought doership actually look like in practice? Not as a hiring criterion, but as something a leader does themselves?

Fair point. I told everyone to demand scar tissue from their advisors. Time to show mine.

400 Hours, 75x

Over the past nine months, working alongside a professor at Harvard Business School, I ran an experiment on myself. The question was simple: What happens when a single person with deep domain expertise uses generative AI not as a research assistant but as a genuine operating partner?

I didn’t outsource thinking to the models. I built with them. Using Claude, Claude Code, and ChatGPT, I produced strategy documents, prototyped platforms, designed organizational frameworks, drafted operating models, created pitch materials, analyzed data sets, and stress-tested ideas at a pace and scale that would have been physically impossible working alone with traditional tools. Over nine months I invested roughly 400 hours of personal time. Not full-time. Focused, deliberate, high-intensity work, early mornings, weekends, the kind of hours any senior leader already puts in.

We measured the output against what it would cost to produce comparable work through a top-tier consulting engagement. The numbers were staggering. My 400 hours generated output equivalent to approximately 30,000 billable hours (of none AI assisted time) at McKinsey or BCG rates—roughly $12 million in consulting value. That’s a 75x productivity multiplier.

I want to be precise about what that means and what it doesn’t. It doesn’t mean I replaced 75 consultants. It means that the traditional bottleneck, the gap between having an idea and producing the artifact that tests, communicates, or operationalizes that idea, has collapsed. The work that used to require a team of analysts, a project manager, and eight weeks of billable hours can now happen in an afternoon, if the person driving it knows what good looks like.

That last part is the key. And it’s the part most people get wrong about AI.

The Multiplier Isn’t the Model. It’s You.

There’s a seductive narrative circulating right now that AI is the great equalizer, that anyone with access to the tools can produce expert-level work. I spent my last article explaining why that’s dangerous when it comes to faux thought leadership. But the flip side is just as important: when a person with genuine expertise and operational instinct picks up these tools, the leverage is extraordinary.

The 75x multiplier didn’t happen because the models are brilliant. They are, sometimes. But they’re also confidently wrong, structurally lazy, and prone to producing work that looks impressive and says nothing. The multiplier happened because I could tell the difference. Thirty years of building companies, selling them, watching them fail, and starting over gave me a filter that no amount of prompt engineering can replicate. I knew which outputs to keep, which to throw away, which to push three rounds deeper, and which were solving the wrong problem entirely.

This is the paradox the experiment revealed: AI amplifies what’s already there. In the hands of someone who has done the work, it’s a force multiplier. In the hands of someone performing expertise, it’s a confidence multiplier, and those are very different things.

A faux expert using AI will produce more faux expertise, faster. An operator using AI will produce more tested, pressure-checked, operationally grounded work faster. The tools don’t close the gap between the two. They widen it.

What Building with AI Actually Looks Like

I want to be specific here, because specificity is the whole point.

When I say I used the models as operating partners, I don’t mean I typed a prompt and got a document. I mean, I worked with them the way I’d work with a talented junior team member who has unlimited stamina and no institutional knowledge. I provided the context, the judgment, and the direction. They provided the speed, the breadth, and the ability to iterate at a pace my brain alone couldn’t match.

Some days that meant generating twelve versions of an organizational model for a platform-based talent strategy, comparing them against real constraints I’d encountered in my own companies, and arriving at a hybrid I wouldn’t have reached through linear thinking. Other days, it meant using Claude Code to prototype a tool in hours that would have taken a dev team weeks to scope, just to see whether an idea was worth pursuing before committing real resources. And some days it meant having the model argue against my own assumptions and deliberately prompting it to stress-test my reasoning the way a tough board member would.

The failures mattered as much as the successes. There were stretches where the models produced work that was structurally sound but strategically hollow… the AI equivalent of the expertise theater I wrote about in my last piece. It looked like strategy. It had the right sections and the right vocabulary. But it didn’t have a point of view. Catching that, over and over, is where the human expertise earns its keep.

There were also moments where the models surfaced connections I hadn’t seen, patterns across data sets, structural analogies between industries, and second-order implications of a strategy that I’d been too close to the problem to notice. Those moments were genuinely valuable. But they only happened because I’d set up the problem with enough operational specificity for the model to work with. Garbage in, garbage out remains the iron law, even at 75x.

The Leadership Implication

Here’s what this means for the CEOs and CHROs I challenged in my last article.

If a single person with domain expertise and 400 hours can produce $12 million worth of strategic output, then the traditional model of buying that output from consulting firms and thought leaders deserves serious reexamination. I’m not arguing that consultancies are obsolete. I’m arguing that the ratio of what you can build internally versus what you need to outsource has shifted dramatically, and most leadership teams haven’t updated their mental model to reflect that.

This has three practical implications.

First, leaders need to get their hands on the tools. Not in a two-hour demo or a corporate AI literacy workshop. In the actual work. The CEOs who are going to navigate this transition successfully are the ones who are building with AI alongside their teams, not the ones who are receiving briefings about it. If you’re making strategic decisions about AI and you haven’t personally used it to produce something real, a strategy document, a prototype, a restructured process, you’re leading from the same safe distance I criticized in my last piece.

Second, rethink the build-versus-buy-advice calculation. A 75x multiplier doesn’t mean you never hire outside help. It means the outside help you hire should look fundamentally different. Instead of paying for analysis you could now generate internally, pay for the operational expertise that makes AI output trustworthy, including the filter, the judgment, the scar tissue. Bring in people who have built in your domain and can help your team build faster, not people who will produce a deck your own people could now create in a weekend.

Third, reconsider what “senior” means. The traditional value of seniority was access to pattern recognition accumulated over decades. That hasn’t changed. What’s changed is the throughput. A senior leader with AI tools can now act on their pattern recognition at a speed and scale that wasn’t possible two years ago. That makes experienced operators exponentially more valuable, not less. But only if they actually use the tools. Seniority without fluency in AI is a depreciating asset.

Show Your Work

In my last article, I challenged leaders to demand experiments, not insights. To hire builders, not narrators. To vet advisors for scar tissue, not slide decks.

This article is me taking my own advice.

Four hundred hours. Nine months. Seventy-five times the output. Measured, not claimed. I’m not sharing this to say I’m impressive. I’m sharing it because this is what thought doership looks like when you stop talking about AI’s potential and start testing it against real work. The results aren’t hypothetical. They’re documented.

The tools are here. They’re not perfect. I spent plenty of those 400 hours correcting, redirecting, and throwing out bad output. But the asymmetry is undeniable. The leaders who engage with these tools directly, with real expertise and real problems, will build things the rest of the market is still commissioning reports about.

The future still belongs to the people who build it first. I’m just trying to show what that building actually looks like.

item.title: Key U.S. Antimony Mine Advances Towards Restart With EXIM Loan
item.description: Perpetua Resources says a loan from the U.S. Export/Import Bank opens the way for a restart of a key Idaho-based antimony mine later this year.
item.dc:creator: David Blackmon, Senior Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Perpetua Resources announced on March 31, 2026 the unanimous agreement by the board of the U.S. Export/Import Bank (EXIM Bank) to notify Congress of a $2.7 billion loan to advance development of the company’s Stibnite Gold Mine in central Idaho. It’s a major step towards restarting a mothballed mine which once supplied 90% of U.S. needs for antimony, a critical mineral which goes into an array of high-tech products, renewable energy, and Pentagon weapons systems.

“Today’s decision marks the final phase of EXIM approval,” said Jon Cherry, President & CEO of Perpetua Resources said in the company’s release. “We’ve worked diligently with U.S. EXIM for over two years on a financing solution aimed at strengthening America’s supply chains, creating jobs right here at home, and fortifying national security." The announcement comes six months after Perpetua held a joint ribbon-cutting ceremony with state and Pentagon officials marking its having obtained final permitting approval to move forward with construction required to restart the mining operations at the Stibnite site.

A Long History of Plentiful Antimony Supply

Though known to contain sizable quantities of an array of minerals, including gold and silver, the Stibnite mine’s reserves of the critical mineral antimony is the main prize the Pentagon hopes to secure from its operations. In documents filed during its years-long permitting process with the U.S. Forest Service, Perpetua estimates the mine’s ore contains as much 4.8 million ounces of gold, 6.4 million ounces of silver, and 149 million pounds of antimony. The overall mining site covers 3,200 acres of land including 3 pit mining sites.

Crucially, Perpetua estimates the Stibnite mine could enhance U.S. energy security and national security by providing up to 35% of U.S antimony needs in the coming decades. That’s an estimate grounded in reality. The Stibnite Mine, first established more than a century ago as a pure gold mining operation, became a key part of Franklin Roosevelt’s “arsenal of democracy,” supplying 90% of America’s huge antimony needs during World War II. The mine went dormant in the 1990s, but Perpetua has spent the last decade-and-a-half working its way through the federal permitting process required to get it restarted.

Cherry points out that the finalization of the EXIM Bank loan moves the ball forward to its goal of making a final investment decision later this year. The company also published updated project economics as part of its release. “Assuming a $4,500/oz gold price, the updated model reports Stibnite’s unlevered, after-tax project NPV of $6.1 billion and after-tax IRR of 32.3% reaffirming Stibnite as a premier gold antimony project,” Cherry says.

An Insulated Stream of Domestic Antimony Supply

The update highlights one of the Stibnite project’s major advantages: The fact that its antimony is recovered along with the mine’s gold production - the two valuable minerals must be separated from one another in the post-production process. It’s a unique feature which insulates the Stibnite Mine from any future effort by the Chinese government to collapse antimony prices by flooding the market, one which helps explain why the Trump Administration - especially the Pentagon - has taken such an interest in ensuring the mine is successfully restarted.

Maj. Gen. John T. Reim, Joint Program Executive Officer Armaments & Ammunition and Picatinny Arsenal Commanding General made the Pentagon’s interest clear at last year’s ribbon cutting. “The Stibnite project currently holds the largest identified reserve of antimony in the U.S. It is one of the largest antimony reserves outside of foreign control," he told the gathering. “This mine offers a secure, reliable, domestic resource for military grade antimony sulfide and is aligned with the Army’s ongoing ‘Ground-to-Round’ assured munitions strategy for establishing a complete domestic supply chain, from raw material access to material processing to ammunition production."

With approval of the EXIM Bank’s loan, Perpetua is one step closer to re-establishing that desired domestic the supply chain for antimony. It’s a goal which has been a long time coming.

item.title: The Best Spring Travel Deals—Save 20% With Booking.com Promo Codes
item.description: We found the best Booking.com promo codes and offers to help you save on hotel stays, car rentals and more. Browse our list of top spring travel deals available now.
item.dc:creator: Elsie Boskamp, Forbes Staff
item.content:encoded:
Whether you’re gearing up for a road trip or jetting off to an international destination, Booking.com promo codes can help you save on airfare, hotel stays, car rentals and excursions. Plus, if you sign up for Booking.com’s free loyalty program called Genius, you can get additional discounts on qualifying reservations. Check out our list of the best stays in New York City if you need some inspiration, or consider booking a getaway to one of our travel team’s favorite all-inclusive resorts in Punta Cana. Below, we’ve listed the best Booking.com travel deals available this week.

Best Booking.com Promo Codes, Coupons And Deals Right Now

Save Up To 20% By Signing Up For Genius

Consider signing up for Booking.com’s free loyalty program, Genius, for access to the best travel deals. As a member, you’ll get exclusive discounts—often between 10% and 20% off—and travel rewards on select stays and rentals worldwide. Genius is a tiered rewards membership, meaning the more you book, the higher your status becomes. As your Genius status increases, you can access even more benefits, including higher discounts, complimentary breakfast on select stays, free room upgrades and priority support. Outside of the Genius loyalty program, you can also create a free Rewards & Wallet account to start earning rewards on purchases.

Browse Spring Travel Deals

If you’re planning out a trip, it’s worth checking out Booking.com’s current offers. This week, you can save 15% or more on select stays. The promotion is available at participating locations across the world when you book and stay this April.

Create A Booking.com Account

Outside of the Genius loyalty program, Booking.com also offers a free Rewards & Wallet account. You can join with your email to start earning rewards on purchases, which can then be used towards future reservations.

How We Find The Best Booking.com Discount Codes And Deals

Each Booking.com deal listed above was personally selected by the Forbes Vetted deals team. Here’s how we sourced and verified each offer.

  • This story was written by senior deals editor Elsie Boskamp. She has five years of experience writing and editing commerce content and covering large-scale shopping events like Black Friday and Cyber Monday.
  • For this list, she searched Booking.com for the best available travel deals. She also cross-checked those deals with other publications, coupon sites and online forums.
  • The Forbes Vetted deals team spends hours sourcing and verifying coupons and deals available at major retailers. Our editors test every Booking.com code in real time and confirm that each deal is active before publishing any new offers. We monitor coupon expiration dates and update this list regularly.

Most Popular Booking.com Coupons And Discounts

  • Spring travel deals: Save 15% or more on select stays.
  • Booking.com Genius deals: Sign up to save up to 20% on select reservations.
  • New customer code: 15% off your first booking with the coupon listed above.

Shop More Deals This Week

  • Away promo codes: Get 15% off editor-approved luggage bundles.
  • Expedia promo codes and deals: Save up to 40% on spring travel.
  • Use these DoorDash coupons to get free delivery and 15% off.
  • Find out how to file your taxes for free with H&R Block coupons.

item.title: ‘Paradise’ Just Changed Its Entire Genre In Its Season 2 Finale
item.description: The Paradise season 2 finale changed the entire genre of the series past anything season 1 even hinted at before this.
item.dc:creator: Paul Tassi, Senior Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Paradise is a fascinating show, just good enough to make it into the critical zeitgeist with award nominations (though few wins) and enough of a hit for renewal until it will finish its story. And it will end in a very different place than where it started. While it was hinted all season, Paradise’s season 2 finale cemented the fact that the series has fundamentally switched sci-fi genres.

The leap? Spoilers follow, though I won’t get into specifics about exact moments in the episode. Season 1 of Paradise was a pure, grounded apocalyptic drama, as grounded as the destruction of most of the world can be. A catastrophic event befalls the earth, driving a selection of elites underground to an idyllic dome with sinister overtones. Outside, we learned, there are many survivors living miserably, as apocalypse survivors do, and they want that to change.

Well, it turns out everyone wants it to change so badly, we are moving into a complete alternate-universe, time-travel, AI-based storyline with reveals in the finale, events that include resurrected children, and potentially resetting the entire end of the world.

I cannot remember the last time I saw a show switch genres entirely like this between seasons. The first series that comes to mind is LOST, a show that seemed like it would be about survivors dealing with each other on a deserted island to survive. But pretty quickly there, we realized this was a magic island, or something similar, with healing powers and smoke monsters. That did not take a full season.

Some shows do this as a core concept. I’m thinking of a series like Search Party, which flipped genres every single season as an experiment to see if that concept could work (it did). But what Paradise is doing here? The best example I can come up with is…Family Matters, the old sitcom which started with Steve Urkel as a goofy neighbor and ended with him cloning himself and going to space. Truly, the closest approximation I can think of.

Are audiences buying it? Yes, yes, they are. Last night’s finale, Exodus, has an 8.8/10 on IMDB, tied for the second-highest episode of the series with this season’s A Holy Charge, behind only The Day, which might be the best rendition of an unfolding apocalypse that I’ve ever seen.

I applaud Paradise for getting away with it. This is not an easy line to leap across, but I understand why they did it, as bunker versus outside on its face is not the most compelling conflict, and this gives it a totally new dimension that sets up a wild third and final season.

Follow me on Twitter, YouTube, and Instagram.

Pick up my sci-fi novels the Herokiller series and The Earthborn Trilogy.

item.title: How Tinder Is Pioneering Safety And Connection To Drive Inclusive Growth
item.description: For all the clichés about dating apps, the real story is often much more vulnerable. In a time when loneliness, social anxiety and the search for belonging shape so many people’s lives, platforms like Tinder are not just changing how people date — they are changing how people find affirmation, community and the courage to reach out.
item.dc:creator: Afdhel Aziz, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
For all the clichés about dating apps, the real story is often much more vulnerable. In a time when loneliness, social anxiety and the search for belonging shape so many people’s lives, platforms like Tinder are not just changing how people date — they are changing how people find affirmation, community and the courage to reach out.

Melissa Hobley, Tinder’s Chief Marketing Officer, sees that shift up close. In her view, Tinder is not just a tool for dating, but a global engine for connection. Whether that means helping LGBTQ+ users find affirmation in places where community is harder to access, investing heavily in trust and safety, or creating new ways for people to meet in real life, her vision is rooted in a simple idea: growth is strongest when more people feel seen, supported and safe enough to show up as themselves.

Why Inclusive Growth Matters

What stands out most in Hobley’s view of the business is how tightly growth and impact are linked. Safety is not a side initiative. Inclusion is not a campaign theme. They are part of the core model.

That philosophy becomes especially clear in Tinder’s relationship with the LGBTQ+ community. Hobley said one of the most humbling discoveries of her time at the company was realizing just how significant Tinder’s role has become in queer self-discovery and belonging.

“We’ve had nearly 7 billion matches for the LGBTQ+ community,” she said.

The number is huge, but the emotional meaning behind it is even bigger. Hobley recalled hearing that many queer users come out on Tinder before they come out anywhere else. At first, she was skeptical. Then she began asking friends in the LGBTQ+ community about their own experiences.

“When I tell you 90% of them said, of course they came out on Tinder first,” she said.

That insight reveals something important about the platform’s cultural role. In cities with visible queer communities, connection may be easier to find offline. But that is not the reality for much of the world. Hobley, who grew up in Indiana, understands that gap personally. In many parts of Asia, Africa, the Middle East, and Central and South America, Tinder is not just a dating app. It can be a first doorway into affirmation, identity and community.

“Affirmation is the first thing that’s important to Tinder when you come in,” she said. “It’s not your credit card. It’s not to just swipe on somebody. It’s to make you feel great about who you are.”

That work is not only cultural. It is measurable. Hobley said queer matches on Tinder are up almost 70% year over year, a sign that the company’s investment in designing for that community is resonating. Tinder now operates in 190 countries, has more than 700 million downloads, facilitates 1.5 million dates a week, and sees up to 3 billion swipes a day. Its scale gives it enormous reach. It also gives it a very real responsibility.

Building Trust Into the Product

Nowhere is that responsibility more visible than in trust and safety.

Hobley said Tinder invests $125 million a year in the area and has launched more than 20 trust and safety features. Some of the most important are designed to intervene in real time, before harm escalates.

One is “Are You Sure?”, which flags potentially offensive or abusive language before a message is sent. Another, “Does This Bother You?”, prompts the recipient when a harmful interaction may have occurred, making it easier to block or report the sender. The goal is not only to moderate behavior, but to reduce the friction that often keeps users from reporting abuse in the first place.

Those tools are being strengthened by AI, which Hobley sees less as a futuristic add-on than as a practical way to respond faster and more intelligently.

“If you’re trying to send a message that’s clearly inappropriate, homophobic, racist, etc., it will not go through and we shut it down,” she said.

Another major step is Face Check, a verification tool that scans a user’s face to confirm identity and help reduce fraud, bots and duplicate accounts. Hobley said it has reduced exposure to bad actors by 60%.

“It prevents duplicate accounts. It prevents fraud. You almost can’t overstate how significant this is,” she said.

For LGBTQ+ users in particular, safety can also extend far beyond the app itself. Tinder’s Traveler Alert warns users when they enter countries where LGBTQ+ identities are criminalized. Hobley said the app sends more than 600,000 of those alerts a month.

That kind of feature may not make flashy headlines, but it says something powerful about the company’s priorities. The same platform that helps people flirt, date and meet also has to understand the risks some of its users face simply by being who they are.

From Digital Connection to Real Life

Tinder’s purpose, though, is not only about reducing harm. It is also about helping people move from digital connection to real-world connection at a time when that leap has become harder for many people to make.

“We are in a loneliness epidemic,” Hobley said.

She pointed to the lasting aftereffects of COVID, rising social anxiety, and research showing steep declines in in-person time among younger generations. For many users, the phone is not replacing human connection so much as becoming the safest first step toward it.

“The positive of Tinder is meet you where you are,” she said. “You’re on your phone. You’re such a digitally centric generation that this is where you feel safer communicating.”

But Tinder does not want people to stay on the app forever. Hobley was clear about that.

“I don’t want you to stay on the app,” she said. “I want you to go out for coffee or go out for a glass of wine or go to the baseball game or the protest.”

That idea is shaping Tinder’s new IRL events strategy, which is launching in Los Angeles and designed to help people meet around shared interests in lower-pressure settings. Dog walks, trivia nights and community-based gatherings may sound simple, but that is the point. Shared context can make connection feel more natural, whether it leads to romance, friendship or something in between.

“We kind of start with this base of real connection,” Hobley said. “Maybe there’s chemistry or a spark. Maybe it’s more friendship.”

Showing Up for Queer Communities in Culture

The company is also extending its values into culture. Hobley described Tinder’s work with designer Willy Chavarria, including capsule collections benefiting the Human Rights Campaign, and a partnership with Safe Place in France, an NGO fighting discrimination and violence against LGBTQIA+ communities. She also called being present for the legalization of same-sex marriage in Thailand one of the highlights of her career.

“We were invited to be a part of this historic day because of the work we do on the ground with advocates and with the movement,” she said.

Where Growth and Purpose Meet

That may be the clearest expression of what Tinder is trying to become. Not just a platform that reflects culture, but one that helps shape it. Not just a product that scales, but one that makes belonging feel more possible.

For a company built on introductions, that may be its most meaningful match yet.

item.title: 7 Risks Of Being Friends With Your Manager, New Survey Finds
item.description: A new survey shows many workers want to be friends with their manager. But the risks, including favoritism and blurred boundaries, can impact careers.
item.dc:creator: Caroline Castrillon, Senior Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Forget quiet quitting. Some workers are taking the opposite approach by getting closer to their managers in the hope of improving their work experience. But new data suggests that strategy may come with unintended consequences.

A survey of 2,000 U.S. workers by JobHire.AI found that 26% are already friends with their manager outside of work, while another 32% say they would like to be. Many report greater flexibility, trust and visibility. But the same data also reveals a deeper tension, including blurred boundaries, perceived favoritism and added pressure that can undermine careers.

Before becoming friends with your manager, these risks are worth considering.

1. Boundaries Start To Blur

More than half of workers (54%) say friendships with their manager blur professional boundaries, making it harder to separate performance from personal connection. What starts as casual rapport can gradually shift expectations on both sides. Conversations that once felt informal may begin to influence decisions around workload, feedback or opportunities, even if unintentionally. Over time, it can become less clear whether feedback is grounded in performance or shaped by the relationship itself. This lack of separation can make it more difficult to have direct conversations about performance issues or career progression. It may also discourage managers from giving critical feedback and employees from asking for it, weakening the clarity that strong professional relationships depend on.

2. You May Feel Pressure To Be Always Available

Nearly half of workers (46%) report feeling pressure to be available outside of work, turning informal connections into an unspoken expectation. A quick message after hours or a casual weekend check-in can feel harder to ignore when a personal relationship exists. What may initially feel like flexibility can gradually extend into a sense of obligation, where responsiveness becomes tied to maintaining the relationship. Over time, this dynamic can blur the boundary between professional responsibilities and personal time, making it more difficult to disconnect fully. Even when expectations are not explicitly stated, employees may feel an internal pressure to stay engaged, which can lead to fatigue and reduced recovery time outside of work.

3. Performance May Feel Less Objective

Forty-two percent of workers say they worry their performance is judged less fairly when personal relationships are involved—even if no bias is intended. When the line between manager and friend begins to blur, it can introduce uncertainty into how feedback is interpreted. Employees may question whether recognition is fully earned or influenced by the relationship, while criticism may feel softened or inconsistent. This ambiguity can make it harder to trust performance evaluations and understand what is truly driving success. Over time, a lack of clarity around expectations and feedback can affect confidence, motivation and the ability to improve in a structured way.

4. Favoritism Can Damage Your Reputation

Only 9% of workers believe friendships with managers don’t affect fairness. The majority say it creates either clear or subtle favoritism, which can erode trust across teams and shape how others interpret success. Even when decisions are made objectively, perception often carries more weight than intent. Colleagues may assume that opportunities, recognition or flexibility are tied to personal closeness rather than performance. Over time, this can affect credibility and make it more difficult to build strong peer relationships. In team environments, perceived favoritism can also reduce overall engagement, as employees begin to question whether effort and results are the primary drivers of career advancement.

5. It Can Create Guilt Around Setting Boundaries

Thirty-eight percent of workers say they feel guilty saying no to their manager when a friendship exists, making it harder to push back on workload, deadlines or expectations. What might otherwise be a straightforward professional conversation can feel more personal when a relationship is involved. Employees may hesitate to decline requests, ask for clarification or raise concerns, worrying it could strain the connection. Over time, this can lead to overcommitment and an increased workload that feels difficult to manage. Without clear boundaries, the relationship can shift from supportive to demanding, especially if expectations continue to expand without being openly discussed.

6. You Risk Confusing Visibility With Advancement

Being closer to your manager can increase visibility and access, but proximity doesn’t always translate into meaningful career growth. Informal conversations and increased exposure may create the impression of progress, even when expectations and performance metrics are not clearly defined. Without clear goals and documented results, it becomes harder to distinguish between being seen and being evaluated. This can lead to a false sense of advancement, where employees feel closer to opportunities without actually moving toward them.

“Seeking connection with authority figures is very human: we feel protected, professionally visible and reassured,” says Artem Zakharov, CEO at JobHire.AI. “However, emotional dependency can jeopardize a career. What you need instead is rapport grounded in clear expectations and documented results.”

7. It Can Mask Deeper Workplace Issues

The desire to build a closer relationship with a manager may reflect a larger issue within the work environment. When 75% of workers say their boss is a primary or significant source of stress, seeking friendship can become a way to navigate that experience rather than address it directly. Instead of improving the underlying dynamic, it may temporarily ease tension while leaving core challenges unresolved. Over time, this can make it harder to recognize whether the relationship is supporting long-term growth or simply helping manage a difficult situation. In some cases, it may even delay necessary career decisions, such as setting firmer boundaries or exploring new opportunities.

Building a strong relationship with your manager can support your career, but the distinction between rapport and reliance matters. When expectations are clear, feedback is direct and performance is measurable, connection can enhance growth. When those lines blur, it can introduce uncertainty, pressure and unintended tradeoffs. As workplace dynamics continue to evolve, the most effective approach is one that balances trust with professional boundaries, ensuring that progress is driven by results, not proximity.

item.title: ‘Marathon’ Earned Its Recent Review Scores, Which Shouldn’t Be Controversial
item.description: Marathon is getting a number of great review scores lately, which is the subject of some controversy. But it shouldn't be.
item.dc:creator: Paul Tassi, Senior Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Marathon has had a wild ride over the past year, publicly, and over the last six years, privately. Now, it has finally been released and has been the focus of intense debate. Most recently, a number of high review scores are coming in from major outlets like IGN, Gamespot and PC Gamer (all 9/10s), which have pushed its metascore up into the low 80s.

There are three sides to this debate, and the side where Marathon does not deserve these recent high scores makes the least amount of sense. We’ll start with that.

1) Recent Marathon critic scores are tracking almost identically with Marathon user scores. The game has an 89% positive rating on Steam (where you must own the game to rate it). It has a 4.57/5 score on PlayStation, the same, if not a bit higher. And both of those are higher than that overall 81 metascore.

The point here is that all the noise doesn’t need to erase the fact that, for what it is, a hardcore extraction shooter, it is a very good game. Changes have improved gunplay and looting, and it has opened the door to some of the most complex, interesting maps in the extraction genre. These scores among players who have stuck with the game make sense in that context.

2) Yes, Marathon is very hardcore and only getting more hardcore over time. The high intensity of these complicated levels, this very tough AI, and players that are getting increasingly higher-skilled and better geared make this an excruciatingly difficult experience for some players. But an extraction shooter, or any game, being very hard and “not for everyone” does not make it bad, just because some people are bouncing off. If you are looking for an ultra-hardcore extraction shooter experience, there is really nothing like Marathon on the market.

3) This is all divorced from the conversation about Marathon being a financial success or having a good enough player count. Things are not great in this regard, which is not an opinion; it’s just how it is. This game took six years and hundreds of millions of dollars to develop, and these sales (~1.2 million, by last count) are not close to what the game needs to offset that. Similarly, its player count is dropping in time, and depending on where that settles, plus Destiny being close to dead, and that is not ideal for Bungie.

That does not mean Sony is about to pull a Concord and shut it down. It will not for the indefinite future, and Bungie’s years-long plans remain in place, at least on its end. But the idea that if a game did not meet sales expectations, that must mean it’s bad, is not something that needs to be joined together. Have you ever met Square Enix? It became a meme that practically all of its very good slate of games, from Tomb Raider to Guardians of the Galaxy to Deus Ex “underperformed,” and future plans were shelved, but that didn’t take away from their quality. This industry now has insane budgets often paired with equally insane sales expectations.

Marathon can be very good. It can be very good mainly for harder-core players. It can be good and still not have sold enough copies or not have enough people playing. Life isn’t fair sometimes, but the idea here is just…if you don’t like it, don’t play it, if you do, keep going and ignore all this. It’s going to be around a while.

Follow me on Twitter, YouTube, and Instagram.

Pick up my sci-fi novels the Herokiller series and The Earthborn Trilogy.

item.title: Why Global Trade Volatility Demands A New Kind Of AI
item.description: Business leaders do see a path to more streamlined, proactive approaches to managing supply chain volatility, and AI is playing a major role in that transformation.
item.dc:creator: Laura Clayton McDonnell, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Once-in-a-lifetime supply chain disruptions are becoming a common occurrence. From the pandemic to the Suez Canal blockage and tariffs to the Strait of Hormuz, each new headline serves as a stark reminder of how susceptible modern global supply chains are to geopolitical, economic and policy-driven volatility.

Navigating this steady state of uncertainty has become a particularly acute challenge in the manufacturing sector, where multinational businesses typically work with thousands of first-, second- and third-tier suppliers across their global supply chains. With each new disruption, these companies juggle an elaborate calculus of raw materials and shipping costs, direct and indirect tax exposure, local regulatory compliance and reporting requirements, and third-party screening and vendor risk management challenges.

Pivoting Isn’t As Easy As It Sounds

By now, we’ve all heard anecdotal examples of manufacturing making quick pivots from China to Vietnam in response to growing tariff threats or looking for more ways to source materials domestically. While these transitions may sound easy enough on paper, the amount of due diligence, vendor screening, training, retooling and compliance-related work required behind the scenes to change even a single major supplier relationship is enough to overwhelm even the most sophisticated corporate finance, tax, legal, risk and compliance, and global trade teams.

Technology has been floated as the great panacea to this predicament. The timing couldn’t have been better. Just as the world was getting too complex to manage, a raft of new AI-powered solutions came to market promising to streamline compliance and automate the hardest parts of the global trade management, tax, legal and regulatory compliance workflow. Unfortunately, as many corporate leaders have quickly found out, automating global compliance in a period of massive volatility is not as easy as entering a few prompts into ChatGPT or signing up for a Claude account.

Solving 3D Business Challenges

What makes these challenges unique is that they are simultaneously highly specialized and deeply interconnected. So, unlike standalone functions such as writing code or extracting data, for which off-the-shelf AI models have proven exceptionally proficient, the unique challenges presented by global trade volatility require both deep domain expertise and broad-based integration across different departments and functions.

Take a single issue like indirect tax exposure, for example. Manufacturers operating globally across multiple different tax jurisdictions need to perform countless real-time calculations of value-added tax reporting requirements in every zip code their suppliers touch. That means when the finance department wants to know what it will cost to switch suppliers, the equation must factor in the big, obvious line items like raw materials and shipping costs, but also different tax exposures, potential real-time reporting requirements and hundreds of other variables.

With each new move, manufacturers face a litany of new compliance questions that need to be evaluated across a wide range of specialized lenses, from tax to legal to risk and compliance, before they can get an accurate view of what it will cost to enter a new market. In most cases, they manage this process across a mix of in-house teams and outside vendors. According to recent research from Forrester, businesses typically manage anywhere from five to 10 compliance vendors across different functions, and 37% say this fragmentation creates blind spots, slowing decision-making and innovation.

Why Embedded, Connected AI Matters Now

The prospect of solving this challenge is what makes the promise of AI-powered supply chain management, tax, legal, compliance and corporate finance so exciting to corporate leaders. However, many are now finding that generalist AI models are not specialized enough to address their needs. And although specialized, vertical AI models show promise in pockets, they remain too siloed.

The solutions driving meaningful results for multinationals right now are those that connect the dots between siloed operational workflows to enable holistic, enterprise-wide views and collaboration between departments and enabling functions. They are both deep and wide, giving individual departments like accounting or risk and compliance the probing insights they need to see around corners and spot inconsistencies, and distilling all that information across core functions to give senior leaders a complete picture of the marketplace.

Currently, manufacturers achieving this level of embedded, connected AI functionality remain rare. In fact, according to Forrester, 54% of businesses say their organizations often lack the required context to complete tasks that are part of cross-functional workflows. Echoing that sentiment, recent Thomson Reuters research found that four in 10 respondents say their companies are exploring emerging technologies such as AI or blockchain to better manage complex global trade functions.

What this tells us is that business leaders do see a path to more streamlined, proactive approaches to managing supply chain volatility, and AI plays a major role in that transformation. But getting to the level of seamless cross-functional workflows required in this space will take more than just flipping a switch or programming an AI agent. The opportunity to truly reimagine complex global trade management and all of its related co-dependencies lies in the deep integration of highly specialized, vertical AI models that connect across enterprise workflows.

item.title: The Best New Restaurants And Bars In America—According To The 2026 James Beard Awards
item.description: From omakase counters and tasting-menu spots to innovative cocktail lounges, this year’s nominees highlight the most exciting venues across the United States.
item.dc:creator: Jim Dobson, Senior Contributor
item.content:encoded:
A great restaurant is about more than just memorable food. It creates a unique atmosphere and hints at the future of dining. This idea is central to the James Beard Awards.

Now in their 36th year, the awards are still among the industry’s most respected honors. They recognize excellence everywhere, from top dining rooms to local bars. Being nominated can boost a career, and winning often brings national attention.

The awards are often called the “Oscars of the food world.” They shine a light on the chefs, restaurant owners, and bar teams shaping how Americans eat and drink. More and more, the awards also focus on bigger issues like sustainability, fairness, and how hospitality helps build communities.

The awards do more than just give recognition. They help shape the national conversation about food. By highlighting lesser-known cuisines and new talent, they keep expanding the idea of American dining. Winners will be announced in June.

The James Beard Foundation was established to honor the late James Beard, often known as America’s first “foodie.” In the 1940s and 1950s, while many Americans looked to Europe for inspiration, he championed American cuisine, local talent, and homegrown ingredients. He supported the farm-to-table movement well before it became mainstream.

Foundation CEO Clare Reichenbach says: “America’s food scene has never been more dynamic, more diverse and exciting. We owe that dynamism to the immigrant communities that lead and underpin this industry… we get to taste the world because of them. We celebrate not just excellence, but the people and stories behind it, those shaping culture through food.”

Emerging Chef nominees were also highlighted including Fátima Juárez, Komal, Los Angeles, E.J. Lagasse, Emeril's, New Orleans, Rasheeda Purdie, Ramen by Ra, New York, Bailey Sullivan, Monteverde Restaurant & Pastificio, Chicago, and Adrian Torres, Maximo, West University Place, TX.

The voting body for the Restaurant and Chef Awards includes subcommittee members and judges who can independently evaluate chefs and restaurants. These members are food and beverage writers, critics, editors, book authors, media producers, food studies scholars, and culinary instructors.

Today’s best restaurants and bars stand out not only for their food and drinks, but also for the stories and identities they share.

California

Ki - Los Angeles

Ki serves Japanese dishes with great care, using seasonal California ingredients and traditional techniques. The 10-seat dining room is simple, allowing guests to focus on the chef’s work. Chef Ki Kim recently won the Michelin Guide’s 2025 Young Chef Award.

Missouri

Anjin - Kansas City

Chef-owner Nick Goellner runs this Japanese izakaya, where guests grill top-quality meats at their own tables. The space is intimate, with just 20 seats, making for a hands-on dining experience.

Robin - St. Louis

Chef Alec Schingel serves his version of upscale comfort food with a prix fixe menu that features Midwestern ingredients and global touches. The cozy dining room has nine tables and a six-seat bar around an open kitchen.

Nevada

Tamba- Las Vegas

Executive Chef Anand Singh brings a modern, harvest-driven approach to Indian cuisine, focusing on live-fire techniques including tandoor, charcoal, and mangal grilling. The space includes a vibrant dining room, lounge, and private alcove.

New York

Lei - New York City

Lei blends Chinese culinary traditions with modern fine dining. Owner Annie Shi and Chef Patty Lee offer regional dishes inspired by classic recipes.

Pennsylvania

Emmett - Philadelphia

Chef Evan Snyder creates a changing menu inspired by Levantine and Mediterranean cooking, blending flavors from French, Italian, Middle Eastern, and North African cuisines.

South Carolina

Merci - Charleston

Chef Michael and Courtney Zentner run Merci, serving French and Italian-inspired dishes in a cozy 26-seat space that feels like a private dinner party.

Texas

Agnes and Sherman - Houston

A joint venture of chef Nick Wong and former DoorDash executive Lisa Lee, this diner offers a casual, cool vibe with Asian American comfort food with a cozy vibe.

Washington, D.C.

Maison Bar à Vins - Washington, D.C.

Maison Bar à Vins is both a wine bar and a restaurant. It offers a curated wine list alongside French-inspired food, all in a three-story brownstone building.

Wisconsin

1033 Omakase - Milwaukee

1033 Omakase is a cozy spot with just 10 seats. Chef Ray Worawit Boonyapitaksakul leads guests through a 14-course Japanese dining experience.

The restaurant nominees show that the dining scene is changing. This year’s Best Bar contenders also reveal a shift, with cocktail programs becoming more technical, focused on storytelling, and connected to their locations.

Idaho

Bar Please! - Boise

Bar Please! combines playful experimentation and genuine hospitality, offering inventive cocktails in a welcoming neighborhood setting. It is a laid-back bar with a serious cocktail program run by owners Adam and Amanda Hanby.

Michigan

Bar Chenin - Detroit

Bar Chenin blends natural wines and elegant cocktails in a relaxed, stylish space that has quickly become a hub for Detroit’s wine lovers. The bar is owned and curated by Nick Arone. Inspired by his travels in Paris and Japan, he designed the space to offer natural wines with handmade pastas and pizzas.

New York

Clemente Bar - New York City

Clemente Bar merges art, design, and mixology into an immersive experience with striking cocktails. Owned by chef Daniel Humm in partnership with artist Francesco Clemente, this art-filled cocktail bar features a five-course tasting menu focused on Japanese ingredients and creative mixology.

Oklahoma

Later Bye - Oklahoma City

Later Bye is an aperitivo bar owned by Brian and Bailey Butler and cocktail expert Steven Idlet. Behind The Harvey Bakery & Kitchen, it serves cocktails and small plates in an intimate 31-seat setting.

Rhode Island

Loma - Providence

Loma pairs Latin-inspired cocktails with a moody mid-century setting that evokes a 1960s living room. Owned and operated by bartender Leishla Maldonado, Osman Cortave, and Yefri Cortave, the lounge highlights their Guatemalan heritage with specialized cocktails.

MORE FROM FORBES

MORE FROM FORBESThe World’s Best Hotel Restaurants, According To MichelinBy Jim Dobson

best new restaurants

item.title: Best Men’s Wallets 2026: 6 Tested Picks With Impeccable Craftsmanship
item.description: Based on extensive research and real-world observations from our testers, we've rounded up the best wallets for men to suit every style and budget.
item.dc:creator: Kari Molvar, Former Staff
item.content:encoded:
A wallet may just be the most important accessory a guy can carry, so it’s important to choose one that’s made of high-quality, durable materials. To determine the best wallets for men, we evaluated various options on the market and enlisted a panel of four testers to use our narrowed down contenders daily. We chose the versatile, classic Shinola Slim Bifold Wallet as the top pick overall. If you prefer a slimmer design, go for the Bellroy Hide & Seek Wallet, which holds an impressive amount of cards without bulking up your pocket. Below, find the six best men’s wallets along with shopping tips from our team of experts.

Shinola Slim Bifold Wallet

Colors: 6 total, including Tan and Black | Materials: Vegetable-tanned Horween leather, faille-lined card pockets | Capacity: 6 card slots, 2 interior open pockets, cash pocket | RFID: Yes

Best for:

  • Leather aficionados
  • Those who prefer USA-made goods
  • A solid pick that will wear well over time

Skip if:

  • You’re looking for a card holder or less traditional style

This streamlined, sewn-in-the-USA wallet from Shinola has six card pockets lined with faille (a silky, textured material), a leather-lined cash pocket and RFID protection. “This standard bifold wallet has well organized compartments,” says our tester. “The leather has a soft and supple texture, yet it maintains a firm and durable structure.”

If top-quality material is a priority for you, you’ll love the natural grain leather; it gains character with daily use and only gets better with age. After testing this style extensively over the course of a year, our tester notes that it’s still in excellent condition, with the only sign of wear being that the pockets have loosened ever so slightly. Still, this hasn’t affected the wallet’s structural integrity, and cards still feel secure in the individual pockets.

The 2026 Forbes Vetted Best Product Awards are here: Explore our 150 top-recommended items across categories after extensive research and testing.

Fossil Derrick Leather Wallet

Colors: 5 total, including Brown and Black | Materials: Leather | Capacity: 8 card slots, 2 slide pockets, 2 ID windows, bill compartment | RFID: Yes

Best for:

  • Those who like a casual, distressed look
  • A great value option
  • Its convenient see-through ID compartments

Skip if:

  • You prefer a more polished, elevated design

Rugged with classic appeal, this Fossil bifold is made from 100% leather that’s been distressed to give it the patina of a wallet you’ve already had for years. “The material is flexible and smooth to the touch,” says our tester. The interior has a durable cotton twill lining and features eight credit card slots, one bill compartment and two see-through ID pockets—it’s the only wallet on our list that has one. Our tester noted that cards are easy to grab, although he didn’t love that “the inner ID flap folds out, which made the wallet feel a little large for my liking.” Overall, if you’re not looking to spend too much on a leather wallet, this one is a good value option.

Ekster Parliament Men’s Wallet

Colors: 9 total, including Steel Blue and Juniper Green | Materials: Leather | Capacity: Main compartment holds up to 12 cards; cash storage strap | RFID: Yes

Best for:

  • Its streamlined design and RFID protection
  • Offering easy access to multiple cards with its built-in cardholder
  • Those who might struggle retrieving cards (it has quick pop-up access)

Skip if:

  • You prefer the look of a traditional bifold design

This smart wallet holds an impressive number of cards and bills (up to 12, although the recommended number is nine), despite its ultra-slim, lightweight design. It’s constructed from leather sourced from Leather Working Group gold-rated tanneries and it comes in a wide range of colors. The built-in aluminum cardholder fans out your cards with the simple press of a button, and the exterior provides RFID protection. This feature might appeal to those with less dexterity who struggle to fish their cards out of a stiff leather card slot. (Our tester, who has owned this wallet for a year, also warns that it’s possible to accidentally release the cards via the button.) Another genius detail: Ekster’s Parliament wallet is specifically designed to fit the brand’s solar-powered wallet tracker card, which uses Chipolo technology to report the exact location of your item up to 200 feet away.

Bellroy Hide & Seek Wallet

Colors: 9 total, including Caramel and Navy | Materials: Premium eco-tanned leather | Capacity: 5 card slots, main bill compartment, hidden bill compartment, coin compartment | RFID: Yes

Best for:

  • Those who want a wallet with a slim profile
  • Its discreet compartment for stashing cash
  • A helpful, hidden coin pouch

Skip if:

  • You prefer a more subtle logo design

Opting for a minimalist aesthetic doesn’t mean you have to sacrifice practicality. Despite its lean appearance, Bellroy’s Hide & Seek wallet features easy-access pockets that hold up to 12 cards. “It’s well organized with plenty of different card slots, and the hidden pockets offer an extra layer of protection for large bills,” says our tester.

This RFID-blocking design is made of soft, durable leather and comes in nine different shades, including less common ones like Everglade (a deep green) and Willow (soft sage green). Also nice: All of Bellroy’s environmentally certified leather goods are sourced from Leather Working Group gold-rated tanneries. After almost a year of testing, this wallet has held up well. Our tester’s only complaint is that the stitching has come undone in one corner, but it hasn’t affected the wallet’s structural integrity.

MORE FROM FORBESThe Best Nordstrom Promo Codes And Deals: Up To 80% Off Hoka, Barbour And MoreBy Bianca Alvarez

Ferragamo Classic Leather Bifold Wallet

Colors: 7 total, including Black and Midnight Blue | Materials: Palmellato leather | Capacity: 6 card slots, center slip pocket for cash | RFID: No

Best for:

  • Its minimal aesthetic (it has a simple designer logo)
  • Men who want to make a style statement

Skip if:

  • You’re looking for a more casual option

Ferragamo’s Classic Leather Bifold Wallet is a handsome, well-constructed designer pick. It’s crafted from Palmellato leather, a vegetable-tanned leather, with a design that feels classic and sophisticated. Our tester’s mother noticed this wallet right away and commented on the sharp style.

There are six card slots with a center slip pocket for cash, and our tester found it easy to retrieve his credit cards and bills from this wallet. “The leather feels durable and smooth—the stitching has held up well and the pockets haven’t stretched out,” he says. One thing to note is that this pick might be more challenging to pull out of tight-fitting dress pants, as the wallet’s profile is a tad bulkier than that of other options on our list. Even so, this is a well-made and stylish choice that elevates any outfit.

Tom Ford Leather T-Line Classic Card Holder

Colors: Black, Midbrown | Materials: Soft grain calfskin leather | Capacity: 4 card slots (two on each side), center slip pocket for cash | RFID: No

Best for:

  • Its soft, pliable leather that makes retrieving cards easy
  • Those who typically carry just a few cards
  • Its sleek, compact shape and design

Skip if:

  • You want a little more capacity in your wallet—this one is especially streamlined

This Tom Ford soft-grain leather card holder is crafted in Italy from calfskin leather with reinforced stitching, so it’ll hold its shape for years to come. “It’s an ideal size that easily fits into my palm,” says our tester. (He tested a now-discontinued version of this card holder, but the current pick is almost identical, aside from holding two fewer cards. It also has a less flashy logo—which might appeal to more people.)

This pick has four card slots—two on each side—and a center slip pocket for receipts or bills. “The leather has an ample grip that stays secure in my pocket, and I can effortlessly retrieve my cards,” says our tester. After 11 months of use, he notes this elegant style has become his go-to for special occasions. “It has truly stood the test of time and is showing no signs of wear and tear,” he says.

How We Tested The Best Men’s Wallets

We researched dozens of highly rated brands and styles that are known for their craftsmanship. From an initial pool, our editors selected top contenders for a hands-on testing period.

Field Testing And Evaluation

Our testers evaluated these selections over the course of weeks—and most up to a year—assessing each based on:

  • Construction quality: The precision of the stitching and the durability of the materials.
  • Aesthetics: The coloration and how the material ages over time.
  • Ergonomics: How the wallet's weight and thickness feel in a pocket during various daily activities.

Standardized Scoring

To standardize our findings across testers and time frames, our panel rated each wallet they used on a scale of 1 to 10 based on three key categories: durability, functionality and style. The final score of each wallet reflects an average of these ratings.

Final Vetting

The products on this list reflect a year’s worth of in-depth research and testing. This long-term approach allowed us to see how the wallets held up over time, and whether they developed a desirable patina or premature wear.

What To Consider When Shopping For A Men’s Wallet

Men’s style and fashion experts Jian DeLeon and Joseph Tang weigh in on what to consider when shopping for a men’s wallet. Keep the following pointers in mind when choosing the right wallet for your needs and preferences.

Material

Leather and nylon are the most popular material choices for men’s wallets, due to their durability and smooth texture. “Leather breaks in nicely, conforms to what you put in it and develops a patina that gives your wallet a sense of personalization,” says DeLeon.

Size

DeLeon suggests putting the wallet in your front and back pockets before buying. “It’s like test driving a car,” he says. “Put your stuff in it and see how it feels, but more importantly make sure it fits securely inside the pocket and isn’t annoying when you sit down.” Tang says to keep your wallet as slender as possible. “A slim wallet can fit multiple cards and minimize the clutter that can accumulate in a larger wallet,” he says. “If there is room, you will tend to fill it, so keep your wallet light enough to carry only what you need to get by.”

Design

“For everyday, I suggest a classic bifold—it’s slim and easy to carry,” says Tang. While a clear slot for an ID and a specific compartment for coins can be useful, too many compartments can lead to bulkiness and make the wallet hard to navigate and fit comfortably in your pocket. Consider wallets with a dedicated space for bills and multiple card slots, and possibly a hidden compartment for added security.

Security

With the rise of contactless theft, RFID-blocking wallets have become more relevant. RFID stands for Radio-Frequency Identification, and many credit and debit cards, as well as passports, now come with RFID chips. These chips store personal information that can be read wirelessly. These wallets are designed with materials that interfere with radio waves, shielding these cards and documents from such unauthorized scans. This adds an extra layer of security for individuals carrying RFID-enabled cards. While not essential for everyone, this feature is an option to consider if you’re looking for extra peace of mind.

Craftsmanship

Look for wallets with neat and even stitching and no loose threads. The overall construction, including how it’s stitched together and the alignment of compartments, plays a significant role in the wallet’s durability and longevity. High-quality wallets often have a superior “build” that minimizes wear and tear.

Why Trust Forbes Vetted

The Forbes Vetted team has researched, written and published a wide catalog of fashion stories.

  • Former deputy editor Kari Molvar is the coauthor of this story. She has reviewed a wide range of menswear, from the best watches for men to the best men’s messenger bags.
  • Coauthor and Forbes Vetted commerce editor Katherine Louie has extensive experience covering men’s fashion and accessories, including stories on the best clothing subscription boxes for men and the best leather jackets for men.
  • Deputy editor Jane Sung reviewed all of the picks and edited this story. She has over two decades of experience reporting on clothing and accessories, and heads the fashion, beauty and travel verticals for the site.
  • This story is updated regularly to keep our product recommendations as current and accurate as possible. It was most recently updated in March 2026 by fashion writer Claire Epting, who has five years’ experience reporting on apparel and accessories.

Our Experts

  • Jian DeLeon, former editorial director at Highsnobiety
  • Joseph Tang, fashion director at Holt Renfrew in Canada

item.title: Elevance Health Latest Insurer To Shake Up Management Ranks
item.description: Elevance Health announced several changes to its management ranks at its health insurance unit and its Carelon health services business that include new CFOs.
item.dc:creator: Bruce Japsen, Senior Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Elevance Health Tuesday announced several changes to its management ranks at both the health insurance business and its Carelon health services that includes new chief finance officers at each unit.

Elevance, which is the nation’s second largest health insurer behind UnitedHealth Group’s UnitedHealthcare, is best known for its operation of Anthem brand Blue Cross and Blue Shield plans in 14 states. In addition, Elevance manages Medicaid via contracts with multiple states and also sells individual coverage under the ACA known as Obamacare. The company also has a growing Carelon healthcare services business.

Like many of its rival health insurers, the company has been battling rising medical expenses from customers in its health plans and its stock price has been hit hard.

On Tuesday, the company, which will continue to be lead by longtime chief executive officer Gail Boudreaux, said the new leadership appointments across its health benefits and Carelon busineses will position "the company
to strengthen execution, enhance operational performance, and drive continued growth across its businesses."

“As we continue to execute on our strategy, these leadership appointments strengthen our ability to deliver consistent, high-quality performance and drive continued growth,” said Gail Boudreaux, President and Chief Executive Officer of Elevance Health. “We remain focused on improving affordability,
enhancing outcomes, and delivering long-term value for the people and communities we serve.”

Elevance named the following executives to new positions:

* Aimée Dailey has been named President of Government Business, with responsibility for Medicare, Medicaid, Federal Government Solutions and Group Retiree Solutions. “Dailey has played a pivotal role in expanding Elevance Health’s government programs, strengthening state partnerships, and improving access and outcomes for millions of members,” the company said.

• Will Feest will serve as President of Carelon Insights, in addition to his role as Chief Operating Officer for Carelon. “Feest has led financial and operational strategy across Carelon, Commercial and Government Health Benefits, and will focus on advancing analytics-driven performance and strengthening enterprise alignment,” the company said.

• William Fleming will join the company as Chief Growth and Strategy Officer for Carelon, responsible for growth strategy, partnerships, and portfolio expansion. “Fleming brings more than 30 years of experience across healthcare services and insurance, including leadership roles at Humana where he drove innovation across pharmacy, home health, and care services,” the company said.

• Darrell Oliveira has been named Chief Financial Officer for Carelon, “bringing extensive experience in capital strategy, financial performance, and transformation, with prior leadership roles at MetLife, Prudential, and MassMutual,” the company said.
• Jeff Plante has been named Chief Financial Officer for the health benefits segment. “Most recently serving as President of Carelon Insights, Plante has led the growth of analytics, payment integrity, and value-based care solutions and brings deep experience across finance, operations, and strategy,” the company said.

The company didn’t announce any departures on Tuesday but a month ago the company said the top executive at Carelon, Peter D. Haytaian, who has been Executive Vice President and President of Carelon, is leaving the company effective May 4, 2026, in “order to devote more time to family commitments.”

“We’re not providing any additional changes beyond what was noted in the press release,” Elevance spokeswoman Leslie Porras said Tuesday morning.

item.title: Google’s Major Gmail ID Update—Protect Your Email Account Now
item.description: It’s been 20 years in the making, but Gmail has now changed forever. Here’s how to protect your Google account from hackers as the big ID update begins.
item.dc:creator: Davey Winder, Senior Contributor
item.content:encoded:
It has taken 20 years to arrive, but at long last, Google has started rolling out a major ID update for Gmail users. As of March 31, Google users in the U.S. will be able to change Gmail email address without opening a new account. This means you can keep your original Gmail address but change it to a new primary address, while the original becomes an alias and all incoming emails continue to arrive. Yes, you will no longer be tied to that potentially embarrassing or inappropriate email address. And that’s a good thing. It can also be a bad thing, as security experts have been warning: attackers are using Gmail address functionality as bait in phishing attacks designed to take over your Google account and your Gmail inbox along with it. Here’s what you need to know.

MORE FROM FORBESGoogle Confirms High-Risk Update For 3.5 Billion Chrome UsersBy Davey Winder

Gmail Address Changes Bring New Risk Of Hack Attacks

Your Google account holds the key to your Gmail inbox and all the messages and data contained within. No wonder, then, that it’s such a big deal when that account gets hacked, as FBI Director Patel is currently finding out after an Iran-based hacking group compromised his personal Gmail account. Anything that leaves your account open to attack must be understood for you to be protected against it. This is why awareness of an important new Gmail update that introduces primary email addresses for billions of users is so critical. Knowing how to change your Gmail address is key to avoiding being tricked into leaving your account open to a hacker takeover.

“If you choose,” Google has confirmed, “you can change your Google Account email that ends in gmail.com to a new address that ends in gmail.com.” While the update has not yet rolled out globally, it has now arrived in the U.S., although the security warnings apply to every user. In fact, it could be argued that those who are yet to gain the ability to change address are at more risk from attack as they are more likely to be looking for a way to achieve it.

You can check, and it’s the only official method, to see if you can change your address yet as follows:

Go to your Google account, select Personal Info | Email | Google Account email, and you’ll see a Change Google Account email option. If there is no such option, the update has not yet arrived in your region.

MORE FROM FORBESYour Gmail Account Will Be Deleted If You Ignore This EmailBy Davey Winder

Protecting Your Gmail Account From Address Change Hackers

Where does the security risk enter the equation? Simple: attackers are sending carefully-crafted, often using AI, emails to Gmail users, from seemingly legitimate Google addresses, that claim you can now change your address and, you guessed it, include a link that needs to be clicked to take you to the security checks and option to do so. The security option isn’t checking the validity of your ID, it is stealing your credentials and using them in an attempt to hack your Google account.

These attacks are not new, but they are now more dangerous than ever, given the ongoing rollout of this feature and the media attention that such a major Gmail update inevitably brings along for the ride.

To protect yourself and your Gmail account from such attacks, you are recommended to run an official Google security checkup that will give you options for securing the account. “Users can protect themselves from credential theft by turning on 2-step verification and adopting passkeys as a stronger and safer alternative to passwords,” a Gmail spokesperson said.

item.title: How Museums, Media And Books Are Shaping Brand Collaborations
item.description: How brands like J.Crew, Dior, Prada, Miu Miu and Silhouette are partnering with magazines, books and museums to give fashion stronger cultural context.
item.dc:creator: Brett F. Braley-Palko, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
The relationship between fashion and cultural institutions has grown noticeably closer over the past year or two. Across menswear and eyewear, brands have been aligning themselves with museums, magazines, auction houses and literary culture in ways that feel more deliberate than a typical capsule collection. What makes these partnerships interesting is not only the branding opportunity, but what they suggest about how fashion wants to be understood. Increasingly, brands are looking to institutions with established cultural authority to sharpen their own identity, whether that means credibility in design, proximity to intellectual institutions or a stronger sense of context around the product itself.

That shift can be seen in projects ranging from J.Crew and The New Yorker to FRAME and Sotheby’s, and in eyewear as well. Silhouette’s partnership with the Guggenheim Museum Bilbao is one of the most recent and relevant examples, showing how these collaborations can become more exact when they are grounded in a shared design language rather than simple co-branding opportunities.

Fashion Is In Its Bookworm Era

One of the clearest developments within this broader shift has been fashion’s renewed interest in literary culture. J.Crew’s collaboration with The New Yorker, timed to the magazine’s 100th anniversary last year, brought a fashion brand into direct conversation with one of the most recognizable editorial institutions in American culture.

High-fashion, too, is getting a liberal arts makeover. Jonathan Anderson’s viral Dior Book Cover collection, built around titles like Dracula and Madame Bovary, plays with the context of reading, using a classic tote shape from the French brand, but redesigned as vintage-style covers of classics.

Prada and Miu Miu have approached that same territory differently; building a community around literature instead of simply looking towards books by way of product design. Miu Miu’s Literary Club has created space for discussions around gender, language and identity, while Prada commissioned Ottessa Moshfegh’s Ten Protagonists as a companion to its Spring/Summer 2025 collection.

Together, these projects show how books, authors and print media are taking on a more visible role in fashion, where the printed word still carries a certain authority that brands are eager to align with.

Beyond The Museum Gift Shop

Not every cultural collaboration works in the same way, with market needs and product development often shaping how far a brand is willing to go. Some operate at the level of merchandise – more akin to what one might find in a museum gift shop – while others aim for a deeper exchange, where the brand draws on an institution’s heritage or cultural significance. Or, in the case of Silhouette, on the actual materials used to build the museum itself.

Silhouette’s recently announced three-year partnership with the Guggenheim Museum Bilbao belongs in that mix. The real jumping-off point for the collaboration is titanium, a material central to Silhouette’s frames and equally central to Frank Gehry’s architectural marvel, whose exterior is clad in roughly 33,000 thin titanium plates. That shared material gives the collaboration a more specific rationale than many projects in this category, linking Silhouette’s lightweight, precision-driven eyewear to one of the most recognizable examples of titanium in contemporary architecture. From that partnership came three new collections: Refined, Titanium Reframed and Pure Visionary.

Michael Schmeid, Silhouette’s CMO, framed the collaboration in direct terms: “It’s really the expression of art as a source of inspiration for design.” He pointed to the Guggenheim Bilbao’s “organic floating lines” as something closely aligned with Silhouette’s own minimalism, giving the partnership a shared design language that feels both classic and futuristic, much like the museum itself.

He also pointed to a more literal connection. Referring to the Guggenheim Bilbao’s roughly 33,000 thin titanium plates, Schmeid noted that they are mounted without screws, just like Silhouette’s frames, which are engineered without screws as part of the brand’s lightweight, minimal construction. But it’s not just the construction or material itself that underpins this collection. Schmeid notes that the color options within the broader collection were chosen based on the way that the sunlight moved across the titanium throughout the day.

“It’s here that we see the real interaction between our brand and the Guggenheim – we wanted to honor Gehry’s use of light and colors on the small surfaces found on our frames. In this way, the constraints made the collections that much more interesting and eye-catching. You find this in the end result.”

A Cultural Exchange

What makes these partnerships worth watching is the way they place fashion in closer conversation with culture itself. By aligning with museums, magazines and literary institutions, brands can give their products a clearer context and a more defined point of view, while those institutions are brought into contact with new audiences. In the end, both are better for it, with collections that push at the boundaries of a brand’s identity and, as the old adage goes, are greater than the sum of their parts.

item.title: Eater Relaunches Restaurant Discovery App To Answer Eternal Question: Where Should We Eat Tonight?
item.description: For those of us who live in large, cities, where to eat can be overwhelming. This app relaunch by Eater sets to answer that existential question.
item.dc:creator: Sylvie Bigar, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
If there’s one question I dread, it’s “Where should I eat?” It isn’t that I feel put on the spot. After all, I have been writing about food, chefs, ingredients and culinary traditions for more than 15 years. No, it’s because all I know is where I want to eat. You? No clue.

For those of us who live in large, cosmopolitan cities, where to eat can be an overwhelming proposition. Begun last week, an app relaunch by Eater, the digital media brand dedicated to, duh, eating, mostly out, sets to answer that existential question. Eater started as a New York based web site in 2005 and was bought in 2013 by Vox Media. It now features 45,000 restaurants from 85 cities and as a brand, is expanding throughout various platforms.

The keyword for any digital ecosystem these days seems to be “community.” The more the user joins in with their own favorites or data describing their dining habits, the more personal the app becomes. But what differentiates Eater from other restaurant apps such as Beli or Yelp is that the mini reviews emanate from Eater contributors and editors. The idea is that users trust these experienced voices more than any algorithm.

I recently spoke to editor-in-chief Stephanie Wu and special projects editor Lesley Suter to get a better sense of what the new app offers.
