item.description: Biogenic CO₂ is reshaping carbon markets, shifting focus to plant-based carbon capture, scaling fast due to lower costs and its role in removal and fossil substitution.
item.dc:creator: Elena Bou, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
The next carbon market won’t be built on emissions reduction alone – it will be built on sourcing carbon differently.

In Decatur, Illinois, an ethanol plant captures carbon dioxide released during fermentation and stores it deep underground. The facility injects around one million tons of CO₂ per year, making it one of the largest commercial-scale bioenergy with carbon capture and storage (BECCS) projects currently in operation.1

Projects like this are not new. Over 75 projects with a combined capture capacity of around 65 million tons of CO₂ are currently operational worldwide.2 A rapidly expanding pipeline could push capacity to roughly 430 million tons annually by 2030.

What is changing is the role these systems are beginning to play – moving from niche deployment toward early market formation. Within that shift, biogenic carbon is moving faster than the rest.

Not all carbon is equal, and that distinction is starting to reshape how carbon is valued, traded, and used across industrial systems.

Why biogenic carbon is scaling first

Biogenic CO₂ is released from biomass through processes such as fermentation or combustion, and is part of a short-term carbon cycle. That makes it fundamentally different from fossil carbon – and economically more actionable today.

When captured and permanently stored, it can deliver net removal of CO₂ from the atmosphere. When utilized, it can substitute fossil carbon in fuels, chemicals, and materials. This dual role – removal and substitution – is what makes biogenic carbon strategically relevant.

It is also the most immediately scalable form of carbon removal. Biogenic point sources typically emit CO₂ at concentrations of 12-18%, far higher than ambient air. This makes capture significantly more efficient and cost-effective than direct air capture, which remains two to six times more expensive and at an earlier stage of deployment.3

As a result, most near-term, bankable carbon removal is expected to come from biogenic sources – a trend already reflected in current BECCS deployment.

The U.S. is turning carbon capture into a real market

In the United States, policy has been a key driver. The 45Q tax credit, expanded and reinforced in the 2025 “One Big Beautiful Bill Act”, provides up to $85 per ton for point-source capture and $180 per ton for direct air capture in geological storage. It also introduced parity for utilization, allowing CO₂ used in products to qualify at similar levels.

While 45Q does not fully cover costs, it creates a predictable revenue stream that improves project bankability. In many cases, that is enough to move projects from concept into financing.

The effect is visible. The U.S. now has around 30 operational carbon capture projects, with dozens under construction and several hundred in development.4

Why policy isn’t the only driver

The U.S. combines incentives with favorable industrial conditions. It produces around 16.9 billion gallons of ethanol annually, generating large volumes of relatively pure CO₂ that are cheaper to capture than diluted industrial emissions. At the same time, it has extensive geological storage capacity – estimated at around 2,000 gigatons – often located close to emission sources.

Where capture, transport and storage can be co-located, projects are significantly easier to execute. This is one reason ethanol-based BECCS projects are among the most advanced.5

At the same time, demand is beginning to emerge. In 2024, advance offtake agreements for carbon removal reached nearly 6 million tons, with a significant share linked to BECCS and direct air capture.

These agreements provide additional revenue certainty and are starting to unlock final investment decisions.

Progress is real, but the system is not yet complete

Yet, even with these conditions in place, deployment remains uneven.

Infrastructure is the most visible constraint. Expanding CO₂ transport networks, potentially requiring tens of thousands of miles of pipelines, and developing storage sites are capital-intensive and slow.

There is also a structural gap in the business model. The 45Q tax credit applies for around 12 years, while the assets it supports are designed to operate for two or three decades.6

That gap must be filled through additional revenue streams, such as voluntary carbon markets, product markets, or future compliance systems, many of which are still evolving.7

In practice, no single mechanism carries these projects. Public policy provides the initial push, private capital finances development, and corporate buyers are beginning to create early demand. Projects move forward where these elements align.

Europe is building the system – but not yet the market

Europe faces a similar challenge, but from a different starting point.

Its net-zero pathway requires not only emissions reductions, but also durable carbon removals – with biogenic carbon capture among the most viable near-term options.

Yet deployment remains limited. In 2025, only around a dozen CCUS projects were operational in Europe.8

At the same time, momentum is building. The number of announced CO₂ transport and storage projects roughly doubled in recent years, reflecting the gradual build-out of shared infrastructure.

The constraint is not CO₂ availability. Around 92 million tons per year of sustainable biogenic CO₂ have been identified across the EU, yet less than 3% has reached final investment decision so far.9

The challenge is structural. Europe’s CCUS value chain remains fragmented, with limited coordination between emitters, transport providers, and storage or utilization options. It is often unclear what happens to CO₂ once it leaves the capture site, how permanence is defined, and who carries long-term responsibility. This uncertainty directly affects investment.

European policy has therefore focused on defining how carbon removal is measured and integrated into markets. The Carbon Removal Certification Framework aims to establish rules for quantification, permanence and liability, reducing uncertainty over time.

This is complemented by emerging support mechanisms for carbon removal as well as ongoing efforts to integrate removals into future compliance frameworks. More broadly, Europe’s bioeconomy strategy positions biomass and biogenic carbon within a €2.7 trillion industrial sector employing 17 million people.10

In contrast to the U.S., Europe is building the system logic before scaling deployment. Together, however, these approaches point to a broader shift already underway.

From emissions to inputs: Carbon becomes a resource

What is emerging is not yet a fully formed market, but a shift in how carbon is understood in industrial systems.

In sectors such as fuels, chemicals and materials, carbon remains necessary. The question is no longer whether it is used, but where it comes from.

The divergence between the U.S. and Europe reflects two approaches to the same transition: one prioritizing deployment, the other building the rules that will define long-term value. Both are incomplete but directionally aligned.

In the next industrial cycle, carbon will no longer be treated primarily as a liability, but as a constrained input — one whose origin will matter as much as its price. The next carbon market will be defined not only by how much emissions are reduced, but by how effectively fossil carbon is replaced.

item.title: In Barcelona, This Hotel Experience Is One You Almost Don’t Notice
item.description: Anima Hotels has been doing soulful hospitality in Barcelona since before soulful hospitality was even a marketing concept.
item.dc:creator: Daniel Scheffler, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
There is a particular moment that happens in very good hotels — not famous ones, not grand ones, just genuinely good ones — where you stop thinking about where you're staying and start thinking about where you are. The city finds you. The neighborhood seeps under the door. You feel, against all reasonable expectation, like you belong somewhere.

I had that moment at Hotel Neri. Early morning, the Gothic Quarter doing its Gothic Quarter thing — medieval stone, improbable silence, cobblestones that predate the concept of adequate footwear — and the light through the window had that particular Barcelona quality: golden and slightly theatrical, as if the city has been aware of its own beauty for so long it has simply learned to lean into it. I wasn't thinking about the amenities. I was thinking about the fact that this building has been standing since the 12th century and somehow still feels inhabited rather than preserved.

That is not an accident. It is the entire project.

Anima Hotels — the family-run group behind Hotel Neri and Wittmore Hotel, both in the Gothic Quarter, and Hotel Sant Cugat on the city's quieter edge — has been building this kind of experience since the early 2000s. Before boutique hotels became a genre. Before every converted palace started describing itself as a curated journey. Before sustainability was something hotels put in press releases rather than practiced in operations. The third generation siblings: Damián Figueras, architect and director of Septiembre Arquitectura, back in Barcelona after years in London and Mexico City; and Andrea Figueras, who came up through the luxury brand world — Desigual, Puig, communications strategy across Europe — before turning to the family hotels. One sibling shapes the spaces. One shapes how the world understands them.

They use the word soul without apparent embarrassment. They have earned it.

What Anima Actually Means - Starting with a Hotel

Barcelona does not suffer from a shortage of beautiful hotels. The city has been design-pilgrimed to within an inch of its life. Rooftop pools with Gaudí views. Lobbies doubling as contemporary art galleries. Thread counts that receive their own press releases. Finding a hotel that doesn't announce its own aesthetic greatness forty seconds after you walk in is harder than it sounds.

Which is why Anima is worth understanding on its own terms.

"Anima started as a family-driven project with a simple intention," Andrea told me: "to create places with soul — places that feel genuinely connected to where they are." The name was never branding. It was a word that kept surfacing in early conversations about what kind of hotels they wanted to make, and eventually became the only honest thing to call them.

Each property develops its own identity through the neighborhood around it, the people in it, the culture pressing in from outside. What ties the hotels together isn't a design language or a logo. It is an approach: authenticity, local connection, sustainability as habit, and a conviction that culture is not a hotel amenity. It is the whole point.

"It's less about creating a 'perfect' hotel experience," Andrea said, "and more about creating a place where people can connect — with the city, with others, and with the atmosphere itself."

Every boutique hotel says something like this. Anima has been saying it since before it was fashionable, which means it was never marketing. It was just what they believed.

The Hotel Architects In The Room

Hotel Neri occupies a 12th-century palace on Plaça de Sant Felip Neri, a small square where shrapnel scars from the Spanish Civil War are still visible on the church facade and the nights are quiet enough to hear the fountain. Wittmore, not so far away in El Born, sits inside a 19th-century merchant's house in a neighborhood that went from overlooked to aggressively sought-after in fifteen years and has somehow remained, improbably, calm.

Hotel Neri was designed by Corada Figueras Arquitectos and Wittmore was designed by Damián.

Corada Figueras is an architectural studio led by Franco Corada and Virginia Figueras. She is part of the second generation of the company. Was trained in Barcelona at the renowned School of Architecture (ETSAB) and has extensive experience in designing both private and public buildings, as well as hotels.

She was the first architect to define the atmosphere and distinctive character of Ánima Hotels, and has designed several properties for the group, including Bauzá Hotel, Sky Hotel, Hotel de Las Letras, and Hotel Neri, establishing the foundations and identity of the company, with a strong focus on comfort and sustainability.

Damián was trained in Barcelona, then at the Architectural Association in London, then spent years in Mexico City — working with Tatiana Bilbao's office, building with rammed earth and local materials, publishing through Harvard Press and in Domus. His influences include Herzog and De Meuron, which explains something about the restraint. Then he came home, to Barcelona, to the hotels his family had been building, and brought all of that with him.

Which means there is no translation layer between the vision and the building. When Damián talks about what a hotel should feel like, he is not describing a brief he handed to someone else. He is describing decisions he made in the room.

"Design is often understood just in the physical realm," he told me. "We believe that design lives also within the intangible." The significant decisions, the spaces confirm, are rarely the visible ones. The proportions. The way a corridor slows you down without telling you it's doing so. The quality of silence in a courtyard. "Light, textures, proportions, how spaces unfold as you move through them — creating environments that feel natural, almost intuitive, rather than overly designed or staged."

The creative process, he admits, has "a certain amount of chaos." They know where they begin but not where they'll end. "Along the way, we weave together customized inputs — responses to practical needs, but also to the feelings and atmospheres we want to transmit." The result is spaces that feel worn in rather than worn out. Harder to achieve than it looks. Worth more than whatever the alternative is called.

Wellness, Without The Program

The wellness industrial complex has arrived in hospitality with considerable force. Infrared saunas. Sleep concierges. Circadian lighting systems. Detox weeks that cost more than a used car and leave you feeling, reportedly, like a slightly improved version of whoever checked in. The vocabulary has multiplied faster than the actual benefits.

Anima's version is harder to sell and easier to feel.

"We're less interested in structured programs or 'detox' concepts," Andrea said, "and more in how a place makes you feel from the moment you arrive. It's about creating an environment that encourages you to slow down, to breathe a bit deeper, to reconnect — without even thinking about it."

In practice: the quiet of the library at Neri. The calm of the patio at Wittmore. Stone and wood underfoot and overhead. Amenities from Roda at Neri and Conscious Botanist at Wittmore — both locally sourced, both actually good, chosen because they work rather than because they signal. Spaces, designed by someone who spent years thinking about how buildings make people feel, that don't rush you anywhere.

"Wellness, for us, lives in those everyday moments," Andrea said. "Subtle, personal, continuous — something you experience almost without noticing, rather than something that's imposed on you."

The difference between a hotel that tells you to relax and one that creates the conditions for it is everything. Anima is the second kind. It helps that the person who designed the spaces was thinking less about impressiveness and more about decompression. A different brief. A rarer one.

Local, Actually

Every hotel in Barcelona claims local connection. Most mean a map of tapas bars at the front desk and a signature cocktail named after a neighborhood. Anima's version involves more effort and less self-congratulation.

"Authenticity is everything when it comes to local partnerships," Andrea Damián said. "It starts with alignment — we look for collaborators whose values and creative approach resonate with ours and with the character of each hotel."

At Neri, this means artisans Teixidors and Tossuda Studio, whose craftsmanship runs through the rooms, and intimate gatherings where makers and guests actually talk — introductions rather than programmed cultural experiences. At Wittmore, a partnership with Terranova transforms the library into an extension of this unique editorial. At Sant Cugat, the hotel functions as a community hub, connecting guests and locals through events that have nothing to do with the hotel as product and everything to do with the hotel as place.

"The goal isn't to use local culture as decoration," Damián said. The goal is integration — meaning-preserving, identity-respecting. The difference, in the end, between a hotel that has local art on the walls and one that knows the people who made it. Damián, who trained abroad and came back, tends to know the people.

The Hotel Sustainability That Predates the Trend

Anima began taking sustainability seriously in the early 2000s — about two decades before the hospitality industry decided it was worth a hullabaloodle. Andrea, who knows better than most how badly the communications world mangles words, is on the subject both principled and faintly amused.

"It was so intrinsically knitted into the initial DNA that we gave it for granted," she said. "It took us quite some time to realize we needed to explain it further."

What explaining it reveals: all rooms and public areas are completely plastic-free. Water is managed through smart systems and rainwater collection. Energy comes from renewable sources. Furniture is reused and redesigned rather than replaced — circularity as operating principle, not certification goal. At Sant Cugat, green tanks and water-saving systems reduce resource use measurably. At Wittmore, rooftop and vertical gardens support urban biodiversity.

Damián's years in Mexico — building with rammed earth, timber, coral, materials sourced from the land around a project — shaped a relationship with sustainability that is structural rather than cosmetic. It got into his thinking before it got into the hotels, which means it got into the walls. "Guests don't arrive to a 'sustainable hotel' as a concept," Andrea said. "They simply feel a sense of coherence. Comfortable, considered, and quietly responsible — without ever compromising the experience."

Not a feature. A disposition that preceded the brochure by twenty years.

On Hotel Luxury, and What It's Actually For

I asked Damián how he defines luxury, given that the word has been so relentlessly deployed in hospitality that it has arrived, at this point, at near-total meaninglessness.

"For us, luxury today is less about accumulation and more about intention," he said. "It's the care, the time, and the thought invested in every gesture — the feeling that a space was designed to make you breathe a little deeper, that materials were chosen because they feel honest, that someone has considered your comfort long before you arrived."

He is, of course, the someone who designed the space and chose the materials. That is not a small thing.

"What feels truly special now is intimacy," he continued. "Places that are personal, human, and quietly generous. Meaningful connections, and the sense that a stay leaves you somehow changed. Emotion over ornament. Purpose over spectacle."

"In the end, luxury is the beauty that comes from craft, from attention, from the invisible work behind each detail — the kind that creates a deeper connection with the place, with the people, and with yourself."

An architect trained at the AA, shaped by Herzog and De Meuron, who spent years building with rammed earth in Mexico and came home to make hotels with his sister — when that person talks about invisible work and honest materials, he is not offering a brand positioning statement. He is describing his practice. There is a difference.

The Slow City And Its Love For Hotel

Barcelona, like most cities that know they're beautiful, has spent recent years being very crowded. Tourism pressure. Noise. The particular exhaustion of navigating somewhere optimized for visitors at the expense of the thing visitors came to see.

"Slow down, please," Andrea said — with the emphasis of someone who genuinely means it — when I asked how Anima responds to travelers seeking meaning, slowness, and belonging over itineraries. "Here it is about quality, not quantity. We invite guests to pause, reconnect, and experience the city with intention."

She knows the communications world well enough to know what those words usually mean when a hotel says them. Coming from her, they mean something else: a practice the hotels have been running since before slow travel had a hashtag.

Quiet courtyards, intimate libraries, rooftops that encourage lingering rather than content creation. Spaces rooted enough that guests feel less like observers of Barcelona and more like, briefly, participants in it. "Belonging," Andrea said, "is cultivated through our close ties with the communities around us. Each hotel maintains an active dialogue with its neighborhood."

This is what Anima does that is genuinely hard to copy. Not the design, which is extraordinary. Not the sustainability, which is serious. Not the partnerships, which are real. All of that can be approximated by anyone with enough intention. What cannot be approximated is twenty-plus years of actual relationship with a city. So there you have it, a brother who trained at the AA, worked in London and Mexico City, and came back to Barcelona to build things that last. And a sister who moved through the luxury world and returned to the same family project. Hotels that have been part of their neighborhoods long enough that the neighborhoods have grown into them.

That is what it looks like when a hotel has a soul. From the inside, standing in early morning light in a 12th-century palace while the Gothic Quarter wakes up around you, it just looks like Barcelona...

item.title: How To Watch The NCAA Tournament Final Four
item.description: What Time Do The NCAA Tournament Final Four Games Start? Streaming And Watch Guide
item.dc:creator: Adam Zagoria, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
The Final Four is set for Saturday in Indianapolis.

A pair of No. 1 seeds - Michigan and Arizona - will meet in the second game of the doubleheader at Lucas Oil Stadium, while No. 2 UConn faces No. 3 Illinois in the first game (6:09 ET).

The games will air on TBS, TNT and truTV. The games will also stream on NCAA March Madness Live and HBO Max.

Ian Eagle, Bill Raftery and Grant Hill will call the games with reporter Tracy Wolfson. Gene Steratore will serve as rules analyst.

Saturday’s on-site Men’s Final Four studio coverage airing on TBS, TNT and truTV will begin with The Final Four Show from 3-4 p.m. ET, followed by At the Final Four from 4-5 p.m. ET and At the Final Four from 5-6 p.m. ET.

The championship game is set for Monday night.

Michigan’s only NCAA title came in 1989, while Arizona’s lone crown was in 1997.

UConn has won six NCAA championships, capturing back-to-back crowns under coach Dan Hurley in 2023 and '24. Illinois was the runner-up in 2005 and has never won the title.

The Big Ten, which has two teams in this Final Four, has not won the crown since Michigan State in 2000.

No team West of the Mississippi River has won since Arizona in 1997.

Follow us on X (@MM_MBB_TV) and NCAA.com for schedule updates and the latest NCAA Tournament broadcast news and information.

item.title: Samsung Galaxy Watch Blood Pressure Readings Finally Come To The U.S.
item.description: Years after being introduced, blood pressure readings are being unlocked for Samsung Galaxy Watch users in the U.S. but you will need a blood pressure cuff.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Williams, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Samsung has finally unlocked the Galaxy Watch blood pressure monitoring feature for owners in the U.S.

You may still have a slight wait for this one, as this is a “staged” roll-out that began on March 31. But it is coming.

Blood pressure monitoring will be available for Samsung Galaxy Watch series wearables, from the Galaxy Watch 4 series and newer.

This is not a brand new feature in general, just new to those in the U.S., where a lack of FDA clearance has stalled its release up until now.

Samsung first announced blood pressure readings for the Galaxy Watch series in 2020, when the feature was cleared by South Korea’s Ministry of Food and Drug Safety.

Back then it came to the Galaxy Watch Active 2. That wearable has long since been discontinued, but at least those with Galaxy Watch 4 series devices — released in 2021 — and newer can get on board.

Samsung’s take on this blood pressure reading technology does not truly act as a standalone tool, though.

Instead, a traditional blood pressure cuff is used to obtain baseline readings, and then used once a month to re-calibrate the results. In-between, the Samsung Galaxy Watch’s standard heart rate reader sensor array is used to estimate changes in your blood pressure.

Blood Pressure: Samsung Galaxy Watch Vs. Apple

This is not radically different to the Apple method, which was introduced in 2025. But where an Apple Watch will simply alert you to signs of possible hypertension, a Samsung Galaxy Watch will produce actual estimates for your diastolic and systolic blood pressure readings.

An Apple Watch does not require the regular blood pressure calibrations Samsung does, though. Apple values convenience, where some might find the more concrete estimates of a Galaxy Watch more satisfying. It also means the Samsung approach is of more use if you already have a hypertension diagnosis. Apple’s take on the tech is not designed for that demographic.

Blood pressure estimates join a growing list of heart health features already common among wearables. The Galaxy Watch series has has an electrocardiogram mode — in certain models and territories — since 2020, and it’s also fairly common for wearables across brands to be able to detect possible signs of atrial fibrillation while using the standard heart rate sensor.

One of the next fields for health monitoring in wearables is the particularly challenging case of blood glucose. Samsung has confirmed it is working on a non-invasive optical-based blood glucose monitor technology, which has been studied for years, but is a difficult one to crack, as Apple has talked about in the past.

The Samsung Galaxy Watch blood pressure feature begins rolling out for U.S. owners from March 31, but don’t expect immediate access as it’s part of a staged process.

item.title: 10 Best Garnishes For Easter Cocktails, According To Bartenders
item.description: From lilac blossoms to carrot tops, sesame brittle to strawberry Pocky candies, these seasonal garnishes elevate Easter cocktails and usher in spring.
item.dc:creator: Jillian Dara, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Easter is a holiday synonymous with spring; its spirit of rebirth marked by pastel-clad candy baskets align with the time when the world begins blooming again.

It’s only normal, then, that our cocktails see a refresh with the change in seasons as brighter, lighter recipes feel more attuned with warmer, longer days. One of the easiest ways to add a seasonal twist or riff to your favorite recipe is by using a new garnish—and Easter proves a perfect time to experiment with what suits your preparation and presentation style.

From carrot tops to toasted sesame brittle, flower ice to fresh herbs, 10 bartenders share their favorite garnishes for Easter cocktails that will carry you through the entire season of spring sipping.

Frozen Lilac Cocktail Cube

At Lucky Cheetah in Portland, Maine, bar manager Couper Jepson likes to freeze lilac blossoms to impress guests at Easter brunch and spring cocktail parties. “It’s a garnish that brings a fresh floral pop to any drink,” says Jepson, who especially appreciates it in a carrot ginger margarita. To make: rinse the lilac blossoms thoroughly to remove all of the dirt. Add one at a time to an ice tray and fill slowly with boiled or distilled water, which will allow for a perfectly clear and centered flower.

Carrot Top Green “Feather”

This herbaceous garnish uses the leafy tops of carrots for a sustainable and unexpected twist for your green-juice inspired cocktails or a snap pea martini. “Most people don’t think to use carrot tops, but they bring a fresh, slightly bitter aroma that balances sweeter spring drinks,” says Deena Sayers, beverage director for The Stand in New York City. “It’s also a subtle visual nod to Easter without being literal.” Trim the tops of the carrots off neatly and treat them like you would a fresh herb bouquet—keep them hydrated and vibrant.

Caviar-Stuffed Olives

This adult-oriented take on Easter eggs is a favorite of John Benevides, bar director for Coda Restaurant Group. “They're a bit indulgent, easy to make and a great way to offer a touch of luxury to an Easter dinner or party without breaking the bank,” says Benevides. Use a non metal spoon to empty your desired amount of caviar into a pastry bag (or cut the tip of a small ziplock bag) lightly packing it into the base or bottom corner. Twist the top of the bag to seal; once packed, lightly squeeze the bag to pipe the caviar into the olive until it is full. “The olives become little briny salt-bombs and are a fun way to top off a dirty martini.”

Herbal and Floral Cube

Add a pop of color to a white negroni with a two-by-two ice cube packed with spring flavors like chamomile tea bitters, lavender bitters, lemon bitters and a few sprigs of lavender. “The herbs and bitters add a fun floral element to any drink,” says Amanda Norton, beverage director at Good Days in Brooklyn where they make a white negroni with a touch of Sevez honey lavender for a delicate herbal flavor that complements the spring cube. Plus, they’re easy to make; just layer your bitters and herbs in an ice cube tray and freeze.

Cadbury Dairy Milk Shavings

For an effortless garnish that feels incredibly festive without overdoing it, The Dead Rabbit team likes to shave Cadbury Dairy Milk chocolate over their Irish coffee. “All in the spirit of Easter eggs and the best Irish chocolate in our opinion,” they say.

Easter Egg Cream

For a real sweet treat, bar manager Valentino Gallitelli from Vista Ostuni in Puglia, Italy has created a seasonal Easter cocktail that is a kataifi pastry nest filled with mini chocolate eggs. Gallitelli forms the nest and bakes it until golden and crisp before finishing it with melted chocolate. “Eggs are one of Easter’s most iconic symbols, representing new life and renewal; using chocolate eggs reinforces this connection in a playful, indulgent, and visually striking way,” says Gallitelli.

Fresh Lemongrass

“The best garnish for an Easter cocktail is a sprig of fresh lemongrass,” says Kevin Stacey, assistant manager, bar and lounge at The Umstead Hotel and Spa in North Carolina. To prepare, trim off the woody bottom root of the lemongrass sprig (about half an inch) and peel away the outer fibrous layers; cut into the white bulb at the bottom of the stalk to release the essential oils into the cocktail and add additional flavor and aromatics. “It brings an aromatic and bright, citrus-ginger flavor to a cocktail, but it is also reminiscent of a grassy field or the green grass we remember from our childhood Easter Baskets,” says Stacey.

Toasted Sesame Brittle

"At Elaia Estiatorio, the Meli cocktail is garnished with a toasted sesame brittle, which highlights the sesame notes from the fat-washed gin while adding a crunchy texture that contrasts with the cocktail’s smooth body,” shares general manager, Mike Donelan. “The garnish also nods to Greek Orthodox Easter traditions, as sesame and honey sweets are classic flavors in Mediterranean festive desserts.” To make the sesame brittle: simply toast one cup of sesame seeds, then simmer with half a cup of honey for five to ten minutes until thick. Spread onto parchment paper, flatten, and cool before cutting into nutritious energy bars.

Strawberry Pocky

Unexpected garnishes add to the delight and whimsy of a cocktail experience, like a Pocky stick dipped in chocolate. “It looks like a dessert garnish while keeping the cocktail light and delicate,” says Sean Mulligan, director of food and beverage outlets at the Willard InterContinental Washington, D.C. The treat also adds height, structure and serves as an edible stirrer. Mulligan particularly enjoys a strawberry Pocky as a garnish for his favorite Little Kyoto cocktail—with gin, yuzu, chambord and matcha.

Pickled Endive

Carlos Cabral, bar manager at Wolfsbane turns to pickled endive to add a fun twist to Easter martinis. “Nestle a pickled endive spear between an egg-like ice sphere and the inner wall of a rocks glass, then pour in a feta-washed gin martini (I suggest Gotha Menegik's Gin, partially distilled with Adriatic seawater),” he says. “Without the typical martini glass, the other parents at the egg hunt will be none the wiser to the fact that you're essentially nursing a big cup of savory gin.”

item.title: Essie Rebrands With A Cheeky Campaign And Pain-Point-Fixing Products
item.description: Essie’s new "Cheeky Luxury" campaign captures the storied brand’s unique position that straddles accessibility and prestige, and their latest products continue that legacy.
item.dc:creator: Celia Shatzman, Senior Contributor
item.content:encoded:
When you can boast about having practically the most popular nail polish in the world on your roster—that would be essie “Ballet Slippers,” of course—it seems like your spot in the lacquer world is secure. But essie isn’t taking their success for granted and is launching a rebrand, dubbed the "Cheeky Luxury" new creative platform, that aims to appeal to younger customers while celebrating their history.

“Essie has a lot of heritage that's very connected to culture and the consumer landscape, but with the evolution of the market today, it's important for these heritage brands to future-proof themselves,” says Melanie Ioanna, Global Brand Senior Vice President of essie worldwide. “Today's younger consumers are all about emotional connection, and when they feel something, they do something. We wanted to nod to our heritage but show up in a more fresh and modern way to build that emotional connection with the consumer. Having the opportunity to rebrand is always an amazing thing.”

“Cheeky Luxury” is meant to convey essie’s unique position as a brand that straddles accessibility and prestige. “We see Cheeky Luxury as the blend of sophistication and witty provocation,” Ioanna says. “What we mean by that is we take our craft seriously at essie; we take nail color very seriously, but ourselves not so much. It's this mindset, attitude, visual world and tone of voice that essie brings to life, and it's also about having fun. We take our inspiration from fashion and luxury, but it's about bringing that to the everyday lives of the consumers with salon level quality, but for the at-home routine. We've always been inspired by luxury; we continue to offer very high-quality products, formulas and shades, but it's important that they're democratized, so that they're appealing and usable at home.” In fact, a recent survey found that 96% of US women say they regularly polish their nails either at a salon or at home.

Essie is kicking off Cheeky Luxury with a campaign called “Color with a Reputation.” “It’s bringing our iconic shades from our original franchise to life as these fun characters that have their own story,” Ioanna says. “One of my favorite things is we're pushing feet in a big way for the first time. We know that 92% of women do their toes with regular nail polish, so it's a huge market segment. It was important for us to tap into that and show through striking visuals from this campaign not only beautifully manicured hands, but also beautifully manicured toes.”

One of the shades being spotlit is “Ballet Slippers,” which was even a favorite of Queen Elizabeth II, with a visual of a ballet shoe with a pedicured foot. “There are playful ways to bring to life these iconic shades that have been so present across pop culture and the zeitgeist throughout the years, so we're celebrating the icons,” Ioanna says. “We're celebrating color. We're bringing this witty provocation through and debuting Cheeky Luxury in a big way through this campaign.”

The global campaign will explore the Cheeky Luxury concept in stores and across social media. “It's super important that we maintain an elevated look and feel—our positioning is elevated nail color with a twist,” Ioanna says. “That means having the highest standards with our product, but also how we show up visually. Our content is very beautiful and elevated, but always with that unexpected twist, which is the essie signature—super approachable, super attainable for the average consumer. We are a mass market nail brand. We know the consumer is doing their nails at home, and so it's that luxury inspiration, but with that approachability as well.”

Essie excels at democratizing trends from the salon. Any new kind of finish or effect that is trending in salons is captured for the consumer to be able to recreate it themselves. For example, this summer essie is launching a new range called Glass Nails, inspired by the Korean glass jelly nail trend. “In just one simple product, you're able to get that look,” Ioanna says.

Their newest product launches and marketing will target younger customers. “Our new products are all very attuned to them; they're all very consumer centric, so they answer pain points or tension that the modern consumer is facing,” Ioanna says. Take Break Fix, a new product that is a liquid nail patch to temporarily mend broken nails. “It's a huge pain point for consumers—you never know when you're going to break your nail, and the solutions that were available were extremely complicated. It was glues or meshes—multi-step things that are messy and take forever. We saw an opportunity to solve that with one simple product, and it's this very innovative liquid nail patch, first to market. That's an example of how we are responding to today's consumers through product. The other way is with our color ranges, whether it's these new effects and finishes from the salon, or beautiful new shade ranges that are inspired by trending fashion collections, like cultural color moments.”

To celebrate these innovations, their content illustrates their social relevance. “We are really modern in our aesthetic,” Ioanna says. “We just launched a range called Gourmet Grocery, a shade range inspired by food and grocery produce. The content is very ASMR focused. We have a shade called ‘Crunch,’ like the crunch of lettuce. This is the kind of marketing and content that today's consumer wants to interact with. It's thumb stopping, it's yummy.”

Essie’s voice has always been distinct, which Ioanna describes as “cheeky, playful, a little bit provocative.” They’re coming back to that ensuring it’s updated it for today's modern age. “Cheeky Luxury applies not only visually and aesthetically, but also with our tone of voice, and how we speak to and communicate to the consumer,” she says.

For the rebrand process, essie did a lot of digging on consumer insights. “What's so interesting is nail is the only makeup category that you actually see on yourself without looking at the mirror,” Ioanna says. “You're constantly looking at your hands. We discovered this consumer insight that's basically summed up telling someone's mood just by looking at their nails. Someone said, ‘no matter what, I'll get my nails done.’ Another said, ‘when I don't have my nails done, I feel like I'm not wearing underwear.’ Everyone has a different way of saying it, but it shows the strong emotional attachment to your nails. That's why it was so important for essie to be that companion and be that brand that's going to be with you and lift your mood, allow you to express yourself and be the person you want to be on any given day.”

Ultimately, Ioanna hopes Cheeky Luxury will lead consumers to see essie in a new light. “We want consumers to view essie as the unapologetic authority in nail color, which is how we define ourselves,” she says. “We're fully committed to the best manicure experience. We want essie color and care in everyone's homes. We want to be the brand that everyone reach will reach for. We're shaking up the category, both visually through our content and our new innovations. This is such a jewel brand, a beautiful brand with a strong heritage, so we are digging into the storytelling, all these huge cultural associations that the brand has, like the queen and all its celebrity fans, and challenging ourselves to maintain that essence, but bring it into the modern era and create that emotional connection. That's been such an exciting process.”

item.title: Six Senses Residences Belize Launches Sales For Overwater Villas, Starting At $3.75 Million
item.description: With just 16 residences on offer, the five-star hospitality brand is bringing its Maldives-style overwater villas to the Caribbean for the first time.
item.dc:creator: Emma Kershaw, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Six Senses Residences Belize is bringing the overwater villa experience to the Caribbean, and they’re on sale now.

Bringing the Maldives-style villas to the Western Hemisphere for the first time, the residences mark a new chapter in luxury travel, allowing guests to enjoy a style of vacation that they once would have had to travel long haul for.

Designed by Arizona-based architectural firm Studio CABAN and developed by CRDG LLC, a privately held real estate investment and development firm with deep roots in Belize, Six Senses Residences Belize features just 16 two-and four-bedroom homes. Located on Emerald Caye, a private island off the coast of Ambergris Caye in Belize, the development offers seclusion and privacy backdropped by pristine white sandy beaches, lush rainforests and dazzling blue waters.

Emerald Caye is well positioned for ocean excursions with the world’s largest marine sinkhole and second largest barrier reef right at its doorstep, and welcomes residents to immerse themselves in local San Pedro culture, known for its dining, shopping and nightlife activities.

“People are looking for year-round warm climate and convenience to a tropical escape with access to unique experiences and excursions,” says John Turley, a Development Partner. “Belize is less than a three-hour flight from cities like Miami, Houston and Atlanta, with many more direct routes from major West Coast urban hubs like Denver and Los Angeles, so it’s an ideal paradise for many American buyers wanting an enriched lifestyle with amenities that most can only find in places like the Maldives or Bora Bora.”

Spacious and airy, two-bedroom homes span 4,387 square feet, while four-bedroom villas feature 7,891 square feet of indoor and outdoor space. Each villa offers two floors, with the lower levels including a full kitchen, pantry, laundry room, living room, sun deck, terrace and pool, and the upper levels feature bedrooms and bathrooms, wellness equipment, an office and balcony overlooking the crystal clear Caribbean Sea. With natural textures and a chic rustic design, furnishing packages from Maed Collective and Huber Design Studio are available for purchase, offering a move-in ready solution.

Six Senses Residences Belize is part of the Six Senses Belize resort featuring 40 overwater bungalow hotel suites, two signature restaurants, full-service Six Senses Belize Spa & wellness center, oceanfront yoga pavilion, private beach club & resort pool, Kids Club, “The Nest” elevated lookout lounge and arrival pier for owners. Drawing on locally inspired and holistic spa therapies, the property’s spa and wellness amenities embody traditional Mayan practices, utilizing the natural remedies of the Earth for both spiritual and physical wellbeing.

Construction is now underway and is set to be completed in 2028.

MORE FROM FORBES

MORE FROM FORBESYou Can Now Stay Inside Karl Lagerfeld’s Former Parisian OfficeBy Emma Kershaw
MORE FROM FORBESHow Seabourn Encore’s Intentional Design Creates An Ultra-Luxury Cruising ExperienceBy Emma Kershaw
MORE FROM FORBESInside A $22 Million Mediterranean-Style Villa Overlooking San FranciscoBy Emma Kershaw

item.title: Cynthia Erivo Charts Yet Another Top 10 Hit From The ‘Wicked’ Universe
item.description: Cynthia Erivo scores a new hit as "No Place Like Home" debuts inside the top 10 in the U.K. following the success of Wicked: For Good.
item.dc:creator: Hugh McIntyre, Senior Contributor
item.content:encoded:
For the moment, the Wicked franchise is in the rearview mirror, at least as far as movies go. The celebrated Broadway musical was turned into two films – one encompassing the story portrayed in act one, and the other detailing what happens in act two. The second installment was released to theaters in late 2025, and while its accompanying soundtrack and several tunes featured on the album performed well, the movie itself did not receive the same critical love as its predecessor. Wicked: For Good was passed over entirely at the Academy Awards, even though it brought in more than half a billion dollars at the global box office.

Months after Wicked: For Good left theaters, and now that its promotional run seems to have closed, one of the two tracks written and composed specifically for the second movie has become a sudden top 10 hit in the United Kingdom.

Cynthia Erivo’s "No Place Like Home" Debuts

For Wicked: For Good, composer and lyricist Stephen Schwartz created two tracks specifically for the film that do not appear in the Broadway show. Co-stars Ariana Grande and Cynthia Erivo both received a new composition to sing, and now, "The Girl in the Bubble" and "No Place Like Home" have both become bestsellers in the U.K.

As Grande's cut returns, Erivo's "No Place Like Home" opens inside the highest tier on both the Official Vinyl Singles and Official Physical Singles charts. "No Place Like Home" launches highest on the vinyl-only tally, where it steps in at No. 8. The same piece of music arrives one space lower on the ranking of the bestselling tunes available on any physical format.

Cynthia Erivo Earns Her Second Hit

Erivo collects only her second hit on both the Official Vinyl Singles and Official Physical Singles charts, and her two smashes come from the world of Wicked. Last April, she joined Grande on "Defying Gravity." That tune, which would go on to win the Grammy for Best Pop Duo/Group Performance earlier this year, peaked at No. 3.

"No Place Like Home" Among This Week’s Top Debuts

On the Official Vinyl Singles chart, "No Place Like Home" starts as the fourth-highest-ranking debut. Erivo comes in behind "Is This All There Is" by Anna Calvi and Matt Berninger, Fatboy Slim's "Retox," and "Intro" by Alan Braxe and Fred Falke.

Erivo may launch "No Place Like Home" in a slightly lower position on the Official Physical Singles chart, but on that tally, she collects the third-highest-ranking entry. BTS debuts "Swim" at No. 1 – the song was made available to purchase on CD but not vinyl – while "Is This All There Is" opens at No. 3.

Ariana Grande’s "The Girl in the Bubble" Returns

On both charts where "No Place Like Home" debuts, "The Girl in the Bubble" returns. Grande's solo Wicked: For Good reenters at No. 6 on the vinyl list and No. 7 on the Official Physical Singles rundown. In the past, it has climbed into the top three on the two rankings.

Cynthia Erivo’s "No Place Like Home" Reaches the Sales List

"No Place Like Home" sold so well on physical formats that the track also manages to fly onto the Official Singles Sales chart for the first time. That list is far more competitive, and "No Place Like Home" barely manages to occupy a spot as it enters at No. 92.

Erivo nearly matches her previously-set career low, which she managed in December 2024 with "I'm Not That Girl," which is featured on the first Wicked film soundtrack. "I'm Not That Girl" debuted and lived at No. 99 for one week before falling away. Several other related cuts, such as "Defying Gravity" and "For Good," have magically reached the top 10.

item.title: The Newest Way To Get A Ride To Your Drive
item.description: Uber has helped solved a problem for golfers — getting to and from the course.
item.dc:creator: Scott Kramer, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Standing at a Midtown Manhattan corner several years ago, I saw two men waiting by a curb with golf bags slung over their shoulders. A car for hire suddenly drove up, they tossed their clubs in the trunk, and they sped away presumably towards a local course. Being from Southern California, I had never wondered before that very moment about how people in big cities get to suburban golf courses. After all, most people near my home find their own ride everywhere. So it dawned on me right then that these guys had to have ordered a car with a big enough trunk for their golf clubs. But how would they really know until the car shows up? And even if they saw on the app that it was, say, a four-door sedan, who’s to say that the driver didn’t already have his or her own things filling up the trunk? Was there even a way to let the driver know in advance they were toting golf clubs?

Well, Uber has finally figured that out for golfers. This morning, the ride-hail service officially launched its Uber Golf option, which is apparently available in the upper left-hand corner of its Services page within the app. The Uber folks say that “while the sport continues to surge in popularity, from standing weekend tee times to getaway trips and celebratory bachelor parties, getting to the course hasn’t exactly caught up. Squeezing clubs on the train and navigating group chat logistics before you’ve even teed off is all too common.” I agree.

And as more golfers like to enjoy adult beverages during and after their rounds, this is a great way to prevent them from getting behind the wheel to go home. Then there are golfers like me who don’t own a car. So now instead of having to ask my buddies for rides to the course or borrow a car, Uber Golf should serve as a welcome addition to my repertoire – that is, once I can figure out why the Uber app won’t actually work on my newest Android phone.

Uber Golf aims to make getting to and from the course easy and convenient, so you can spend more time focusing on your backswing. This new seasonal offering lets riders reserve a ride for up to four players and their clubs. You just book ahead – Uber Golf is part of Uber Reserve, so your ride to and from the course can be reserved up to 90 days in advance. Then you tap the Uber Golf tile in the app and reserve an UberX (up to two guests with two sets of clubs) or UberXL (up to four guests with four sets of clubs). And for any golfers who want to keep their swing sharp beyond the course, Uber has teamed up with Topgolf to give Uber One members 20 percent off Game Play. Starting later this spring, Uber One members can redeem the discount by navigating to the "Account" tab within the Uber app and selecting the "Uber One" tile. The unique code can be applied towards bay reservations made on Topgolf's app or website, as well as walk-ins.

item.title: 3 Ways Visionary Thinking Is Reshaping The Future Of Exclusive Experiences
item.description: Exclusive experiences are about personalization, immersion and intelligent orchestration at scale. Check out more and what action steps to take.
item.dc:creator: David Henkin, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Private tours. Backstage passes. Invitation-only events. Exclusive experiences have long been a defining symbol of status and luxury, giving participants unique access that few others get to enjoy.

Today, visionary thinking is rewriting that definition. The new frontier of exclusive experiences is not just about scarcity; it is about personalization, immersion and intelligent orchestration at scale.

What is driving this shift? A powerful convergence of artificial intelligence, immersive technologies and bold leadership thinking. The most forward-looking organizations are not simply adopting new tools but reimagining what it means to create moments that feel rare, meaningful and uniquely tailored.

Here are three ways visionary thinking is reshaping exclusive experiences, and what leaders should do about it.

1. Access Over Ownership

There is a growing desire to spend on experiences, rather than material goods, across all levels of society. What started as pent-up demand that lingered after the pandemic has evolved into a lasting trend, especially among Gen Z, which tends to view experiences like dining out or attending concerts as necessities.

As a result, there has been an increased focus on exclusive access, rather than outright ownership, in recent years — including with product purchases and other areas outside the typical luxury sphere.

For example, in the video game industry, a growing practice is to release games still in development as “early access” on Steam, allowing a select subset of gamers to play those games and give feedback to developers before the games are officially released. For many, this creates a unique experience where they get to potentially help shape the final product.

Some major companies have even found that customers are willing to pay a premium for early access. For its release of Madden 26, EA sold Deluxe Editions and bundles that allowed players to enjoy the finished game one week before its official release, alongside other exclusive perks.

By emphasizing access, these developers are taking some of the traditional “perks” of exclusive experiences (such as insider perspectives or proximity to influence) and applying them in a new format.

2. Taking Personalization Even Deeper

In an age when AI is being used to create hyper-focused marketing, many business models that focus on delivering exclusive experiences are relying on the human touch to create high-end, rewarding experiences that cater to the specific needs and interests of the individual.

As Chad Newbold, founder and CEO of The Reserve Collection, explained in an interview with MSN, “Curated travel within a membership program allows members to enjoy experiences that go beyond simply reaching a destination. This may include private dinners with top chefs, an exclusive behind-the-scenes guided tour to a cultural site or getting the best seats in the house at a sporting or cultural event. With membership travel, you get access to a full range of exciting activities that would be incredibly time-consuming and difficult to plan out on your own.”

Delivering this level of experience begins with concierge professionals meeting with travel members to better understand their travel and activity preferences. This personal touch then allows the concierge teams to develop personalized travel plans that blend convenience, luxury and even a hint of surprise.

With AI and data, these types of concierge services can take personalization even further, leveraging vast data resources to offer an even deeper level of personalization for itineraries and upgrades, even predicting participants’ wants and needs before they are communicated for a comprehensive luxury experience.

3. Leveraging Tech For Frictionless Experiences

Gaining access to exclusive experiences has often been a cumbersome process. Historically, for example, guests at the Academy Awards have had to pass through three separate security perimeters before getting to the red carpet. Or simply consider the lengthy security and check-in lines at airports, which can add stress and disrupt travel.

While advancing technology is not going to eliminate the need for physical security at high-level events like the Academy Awards, there are many ways in which it is poised to enhance experiences for a more streamlined and frictionless experience.

For example, geofencing can be used to create invisible security layers that are less intrusive. Biometrics and encrypted profiles can eliminate the need for visible scanning or check-ins, and could even be used to help predict and cater to individualized preferences when a guest arrives at the event. For example, tools that track individual guest preferences can allow human staff to bring a guest’s favorite drink before they even put in a request.

As technology helps synchronize the behind-the-scenes logistics, human staff are better able to focus on the hospitality aspects of the experience to deliver something even more memorable.

Action Steps For Leaders And Organizations

Vision without execution is just theater. Consider these action steps:

  1. Start with the experience, not the technology. Map your highest-value customer journeys and identify where personalization, immersion or adaptability can create differentiated value.
  2. Build AI as a core capability. Treat AI not as a bolt-on tool, but as an embedded team member integrated into decision-making, design and delivery.
  3. Invest in data foundations. High-quality, ethically sourced data is the fuel for personalization. Without it, even the best AI will fall short.
  4. Design for human + machine collaboration. The winning formula is not AI versus humans; it is AI amplifying human creativity, empathy and judgment.
  5. Pilot “phygital” experiences (physical+digital). Experiment with immersive formats that blend physical and digital touchpoints. Start small but think big.
  6. Prioritize trust and transparency. Make privacy, explainability and user control central to your strategy. Trust will be a defining competitive advantage.

Turning Experiences Into Something Truly Innovative

Visionary thinking is not just reshaping the future of exclusive experiences. It is redefining what exclusivity means altogether. Whether a luxury vacation or early access to a restaurant or video game, exclusive experiences have become a powerful currency in today’s market. With visionary thinking and a tech-enabled approach, brands and service providers have greater potential to deliver memorable experiences at scale, while also achieving an ultra-personalized approach.

When guests and customers feel like they have been deeply understood and that their needs have been catered to and even anticipated, they might not notice the innovation that went on behind the scenes. But they will remember how effortless, exclusive and enjoyable the entire experience felt from start to finish.

item.title: Blue Jays Make Roster Change After Cody Ponce Injury
item.description: The Toronto Blue Jays added a new player to the big-league roster shortly after Cody Ponce went down with an injury.
item.dc:creator: Peter Chawaga, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
After a disappointing World Series defeat last year, the Toronto Blue Jays emerged with a clear focus for their roster in the offseason.

The team added frontline starter Dylan Cease and veteran reliever Tyler Rogers as it reinforced its pitching staff alongside returns from former Cy Young Award winners Shane Bieber and Max Scherzer.

The emphasis on pitching depth also included the addition of Cody Ponce, who had left major league baseball in 2021 after a rough season with the Pittsburgh Pirates only to reinvent himself as a professional in Asia, winning South Korea’s KBO League Most Valuable Player Award last year.

“In 2025, his whole baseball world shot into the stratosphere with unprecedented dominance. The California native finished the year with a 17-1 record, 1.80 ERA and 252 Ks, setting a KBO record by striking out 18 batters in a single game and (was) ultimately named league MVP,” Rose DiManno wrote for the Toronto Star. “The Jays certainly took notice, luring him to Toronto with a three-year, $30-million (U.S.) deal with the assumption he could transfer those kinds of numbers, or thereabouts, back to North America.”

MORE FROM FORBESMets’ 10-Year Veteran Responds To Disgruntled Phillies FansBy Peter Chawaga

Toronto Blue Jays’ Cody Ponce Endures Brutal Return To MLB

As a result, even among a pitching staff of exciting talent, Ponce’s debut on Monday was highly anticipated. But it did not go as he or the team would have hoped.

In his first big-league start since 2021, Ponce left the game in the third inning with a concerning knee injury. He tried to field a ball hit by Colorado Rockies outfielder Jake McCarthy, but his right leg buckled and he fell to the infield dirt. He was carted off the field and manager John Schneider indicated that the team was hoping for the best as he underwent further evaluation.

MORE FROM FORBESBlue Jays’ New $60 Million Star Sends 5-Word Vladimir Guerrero Jr MessageBy Peter Chawaga

Toronto Blue Jays Add New Pitcher To Big-League Roster After Cody Ponce Injury

However, a personnel change made by the team shortly after the game suggested they could be anticipating an injury list stint for Ponce.

“The Toronto Blue Jays have recalled right-hander pitcher Lázaro Estrada from Buffalo, Triple-A,” Major League Baseball insider Francys Romero reported on X, formerly Twitter. “He is expected to join the team (on Tuesday). Estrada, 26, threw 2.2 scoreless innings with 3 strikeouts on Friday in Triple-A Buffalo.”

Though the Blue Jays reloaded their rotation with an apparent surplus of options before the season started, their depth is already being tested. Bieber, Jose Berrios and Trey Yesavage opened the year on the injured list. If Ponce has to miss significant time, the team will need to rely on Estrada for some starts as their more proven pitchers recover.

item.title: Trend Setters For Criminal Justice’s Fair Chance Month
item.description: April is Fair Chance Month where we reflect on some of the positive outcomes of people who went to prison and then came back even better.
item.dc:creator: Walter Pavlo, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
April is noted as being Fair Chance Month (also known as Second Chance Month), a recognition of those who face and overcome many of the challenges associated with a felony in the United States. The National Association of Criminal Defense Attorneys (NACDL) has a resource page dedicated to the month and has been a proponent of change for those who have collateral consequences for years beyond a prison term. NACDL’s President, Michael Heiskell, released a statement, “April marks Second Chance Month, a time to reflect on the meaning of redemption, hope and opportunity for those seeking to rebuild their lives.”

Notable Second Chances

Each year we hear of amazing comeback stories. While appreciating that there are victims to many crimes, the best outcome is to restore those victims as much as possible while having the person incarcerated return as a more productive member of society that never returns to prison.

Today, the Deputy Director of the Federal Bureau of Prisons (BOP), Josh Smith, is someone with a federal felony for drug crimes but is now a senior leader at the BOP. Smith left prison and became a successful entrepreneur and was pardoned by President Donald Trump in 2020. Smith has shown dedication to supporting BOP staff while trying to point the agency out of its troubled past.

Also of note is Alice Johnson, the Trump appointed Pardon Czar, who was serving a Life sentence for drug charges. Johnson has become the face of second chances as she reviews and makes recommendations on a pardon application process that has been less than fair to many over the years. Rather than take her own pardon and move on with life, at 70 years old she is giving back by helping others regain their lives through receipt of a pardon.

Musical Return

On April 9, 2026, two-time Grammy Award Winner Irvin Mayfield will keynote the 6th Annual National Returning Citizens Conference (NRCC) in Jacksonville, FL (April 7-10, 2026) with an unprecedented one-man-show — a solo musical keynote speech that debuts his role as spokesman for the HumanWatch Foundation, a 501c3 organization that works to eradicate isolation caused by inequitable systems, such as the carceral system. Mayfield was sentenced to 18 months in prison in 2021 for fraud but has since revived his career and pushes for change in criminal justice.

The person who invited Mayfield to the event and head of NRCC is Carlyle I. Holder, a proven career spanning more than 42 years in criminal justice. However, he is not a former inmate, he is a retired BOP warden. Over his 27 plus years of distinguished service with the BOP, he served 23 of those years as supervisor, manager, and executive. He was a Chief Executive Officer (Warden) for 12 years managing the largest Pre-Trial and Correctional Complex facilities in the BOP. Now he is focusing on making opportunities for those who want to take advantage of a second chance.

Telling A Story

Some of those who have returned from prison have used their darkest days of poor decision making to provide a cautionary tale for others. Tom Hardin, worked on Wall Street and became one of the FBI’s most important informants in breaking the massive insider trading ring in Operation Perfect Hedge. Now, he’s a best selling author telling hedge funds and financial institutions about his mistakes and how best to avoid them.

Rashmi Airan was a successful attorney then became embroiled in a mortgage scheme that sent her to prison. Now she not only uses her prison experience to teach others, she is a leadership coach that presents skills to be better professionals and a better person.

The list of those who have gone from prison to success are many and I’ve tried to write about them here as much as possible. Often, many of these speakers like Hardin and Airan are on stage with leading law enforcement and compliance professionals, putting them on equal footing with those who are teaching business leaders.

What We Can Learn

April’s recognition as Fair Chance Month is more than symbolic. It is a reminder that some of the most impactful people in our communities are those who have had to rebuild their lives from the ground up after incarceration. These are individuals who have faced consequences, accepted responsibility, and then made the difficult choice to move forward with purpose.

What stands out about people who succeed after prison is not that they avoided mistakes. It is that they refused to be defined by them. For many, incarceration becomes a turning point rather than an ending. They return home with a different perspective, a deeper sense of accountability, and often a commitment to ensure that others do not follow the same path.

Across the country, we see formerly incarcerated individuals stepping into roles that matter. They mentor young people who are at risk. They build businesses that create jobs for others with records. They advocate for smarter policies and stronger communities. They become fathers, mothers, leaders, and neighbors who understand both the consequences of poor decisions and the value of second chances.

There is also a unique credibility that comes with lived experience. When someone who has been through the system speaks to a young person or someone struggling, it carries weight. It is not theory. It is real. And that authenticity allows them to reach people in ways that traditional systems often cannot.

Fair Chance Month is an opportunity to recognize that redemption is not just possible, it is happening every day. It challenges us to look beyond a person’s worst moment and instead consider what they have done since. It also calls on employers, communities, and policymakers to create pathways that allow that transformation to continue.

At its core, this is about more than second chances. It is about what happens when people are given the opportunity to contribute, to lead, and to give back. Many of those who once took from society are now among those giving the most.

That is not just redemption. That is impact.

item.title: 17 Grocery Store Wines Worth Buying Right Now
item.description: From under-$12 bargains to bottles for gifting, these 17 grocery store wines prove the supermarket aisle can still deliver real value and reliable quality.
item.dc:creator: Devin Parr, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Most grocery stores aren’t necessarily known for their highly curated wine sections. In fact, “grocery store wine” has almost become synonymous for mass-produced plonk—at least among a certain kind of wine consumer. Of course, most wine enthusiasts would rather browse the shelves of an independent wine shop than grab one of the usual suspects lined up next to the deli meats.

Alas, the real world doesn’t always work that way. And in the real world, you find yourself at 6:12pm needing something for the impromptu dinner you’re hosting, and the closest place is your local supermarket chain.

As much as I personally love a tiny gem of a neighborhood wine shop—and am also a heavy user of wine.com’s Stewardship program—I, too, sometimes have to hit the nearest Ralph’s for a quick bottle. And while it will never deliver the romance of a hand-sold bottle from the Canary Islands, your local grocery store can absolutely deliver a crisp white for takeout Thai, an easy-drinking red for weeknight pasta, or a last-minute host gift that actually looks like you put some thought into it.

Of course, not all grocery store wine is created equal. But it’s also not the wasteland wine snobs like to pretend it is. Sure, it may not be the place to hunt for a funky, skin contact white, but there are some bottles that punch well above their weight class if you know what to look for. Some are cheap and cheerful. Others are good enough to grab when you need an emergency onion and boozy birthday gift at the same time.

Here are 17 of my favorite all-stars at varying price points, generally available nationwide at many popular stores. Prices reflect averages and may vary by state and retailer.

$12 and Under

This section is where the grocery store has the most to prove. It’s also where a few bottles really do overdeliver.

La Vieille Ferme Rosé, France, $8

Affectionately known as “the chicken wine” thanks to the little rooster on the front, this is exactly what inexpensive rosé should be. It’s dry, juicy and quaffable—a total porch-pounder, as the kids these days like to say.

Mezzacorona Pinot Grigio, Trentino, $9

While the best pinot grigios can be elegant and mineral-driven, most of the cheap stuff is watery and forgettable. Mezzacorona’s bottling from Trentino in northern Italy is sustainably-produced and vibrant, proving that the category can still deliver real value.

Château Ste. Michelle Dry Riesling, Columbia Valley, $9

This balanced wine makes a strong case for serious, and seriously affordable, grocery store Riesling. From one of Washington state’s foundational producers, it’s an especially smart bottle to keep around for takeout Thai, sushi, Vietnamese or Indian.

Trapiche Oak Cask Malbec, Mendoza, $9

This is a reliable, balanced red that brings more flavor and structure than many reds in its lane. It’s smooth, broadly appealing, and a much better weeknight option than an overworked bargain cabernet.

Pine Ridge Chenin Blanc-Viognier, Clarksburg & Lodi, $12

I buy this wine often—perhaps more than any other white wine. Not only is it reasonably priced, but it’s got great cross-appeal for those who like crisp and bright, and those who like their whites with a bit more softness and succulence.

Under $20

For many shoppers this is the real sweet spot of grocery store wines. Bottles here feel fairly-priced yet distinct, making great mid-week wines as well as solid selections for entertaining.

Banfi Centine Red Blend, Tuscany, $13

A Tuscan blend from one of Italy’s best-known wine families, this is an easygoing, everyday wine that still carries a distinctly Italian personality. It’s the perfect selection for when you want something food friendly and a little less generic than the average supermarket red blend.

Coppola Diamond Collection Shimmer, California, $13

Shimmer takes the zest of sauvignon blanc and softens in just a touch with some pinot grigio, making for a white that is easy-drinking without being boring. At just 11% alcohol, it’s also a smart choice for anyone who wants something light and crushable.

Benziger Family Winery Sauvignon Blanc, California, $16

While Benziger’s portfolio has plenty of premium and vineyard-designate selections, this entry level sauvignon blanc is a strong grocery store buy. It’s got classic citrus, apple and melon flavors, and a mouth-watering freshness that makes it the perfect accompaniment to a casual spread of salty snacks or a seafood dinner.

J. Lohr South Ridge Syrah, Paso Robles, $13

There’s a reason J. Lohr wines are so ubiquitous: they reliably offer immense quality for the price. This syrah is consistently recognized by critics and media as a great buy, with plenty of concentration and a broadly likeable flavor profile. Plus, syrah is often the unsung (and unfairly overshadowed) hero of the dinner table.

Juggernaut Sonoma Coast Chardonnay, Sonoma Coast, $16

Juggernaut’s dramatic labels may catch your eye, but the pricing is what makes the bottle worth a second look. This Sonoma Coast chardonnay is balanced and bright, with just enough richness to satisfy fans of the California style without being heavy-handed.

DAOU Cabernet Sauvignon, Paso Robles, $19

Like all of DAOU’s portfolio, this cabernet punches above its price point in both profile and presentation. It’s a polished Paso cab that is equally at home as a treat at your own table as it is a perfect wine gift.

Over $20

Not every grocery store wine over $20 is worth the splurge. These are all wines with a sense of place or a nice backstory to justify it. They also won’t give you away for buying them right next to the bread aisle.

Cloudy Bay Sauvignon Blanc, Marlborough, $32

The wine that put New Zealand wines on the map, Cloudy Bay still carries true category significance. It’s a step up from your everyday sipper that holds both wine industry significance and broad consumer appeal, making it as likely to impress your wine geek friends as your serial sauvy b drinkers.

Frank Family Carneros Chardonnay, Carneros, $32

Frank Family has long been a noteworthy Napa name, and this chardonnay delivers that richer California style with elegance and balance. It’s a great example of a grocery store selection that feels truly elevated rather than just the wine you have to ask the store clerk to get a key for.

Domaine Carneros Brut, Carneros, $33

Founded by the family behind Champagne Taittinger, Domaine Carneros delivers traditional method sparkling wine credibility in California style. Close your eyes and you are indeed at a French Château, not grabbing a bottle from your local grocery store.

Sequoia Grove Cabernet Sauvignon, Napa Valley, $45

A longtime Napa Valley icon with deep roots in Rutherford, Sequoia Grove makes cabernet sauvignon in a timeless style that brings a real sense of place rather than mere California bravado. This is an elegant selection that will no doubt impress any gift recipient—that is, if you choose to share it.

item.title: Google Finds Quantum Computers Could Break Bitcoin Sooner Than Expected
item.description: Google published a paper on March 31 that states that Bitcoin's cryptography could be impacted by quantum computing sooner than previously stated.
item.dc:creator: Javier Bastardo, Contributor
item.content:encoded:
Google just told the Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies industry it has less time than it thought to prepare for the quantum computing influence. In a whitepaper published March 31, Google Quantum AI researchers demonstrated that breaking the elliptic curve cryptography protecting bitcoin, ether and most major cryptocurrencies could require fewer than 500,000 physical qubits on a superconducting quantum computer. That’s roughly a 20 times reduction from prior estimates, which pegged the figure in the millions.

The paper carries serious institutional weight. Its coauthors include Justin Drake of the Ethereum Foundation, Dan Boneh of Stanford, and six Google Quantum AI researchers led by Ryan Babbush and Hartmut Neven. Google says it engaged with the U.S. government before publishing and names Coinbase, the Stanford Institute for Blockchain Research, and the Ethereum Foundation as collaborators.

It’s important to understand that no quantum computer can execute this attack today. Google’s most advanced chip, Willow, has 105 qubits. But the distance between current hardware and a machine capable of cracking bitcoin’s cryptography is shrinking faster than projected.

"My confidence in q-day by 2032 has shot up significantly," Drake, who joined the paper as a late coauthor, wrote on X. He estimates at least a 10% chance that a quantum computer recovers a private key from an exposed public key by that year.

Why Quantum Computing Threatens Cryptocurrency

Bitcoin’s security relies on a single mathematical assumption: that deriving a private key from a public key is computationally impossible for any existing machine. The specific math is called the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem. Every time someone sends bitcoin, they reveal their public key. A classical computer would need longer than the age of the universe to reverse-engineer the private key from it.

But quantum computers break that assumption. An algorithm published by mathematician Peter Shor in 1994 solves ECDLP exponentially faster than any classical approach. A quantum computer with enough stable and error-corrected qubits running Shor’s algorithm could derive a private key, forge a digital signature and drain a wallet.

The quantum computing influence won’t impact Bitcoin’s proof-of-work mining, which uses the SHA-256 hash function. Drake put it clearly: "Commercially-viable Bitcoin PoW via Grover’s algorithm is not happening any time soon. We’re talking decades, possibly centuries away." The vulnerability targets the digital signature schemes: ECDSA and Schnorr, both built on the secp256k1 elliptic curve.

Google’s whitepaper points out that cryptocurrencies are uniquely exposed among systems that rely on this type of cryptography. Blockchains use elliptic curve keys that are almost an order of magnitude smaller than RSA keys at comparable security levels, meaning a smaller quantum computer can crack them. And unlike traditional finance, which layers multiple safeguards, blockchains offer no recourse against fraudulent transactions. One forged signature could mean irreversible theft.

Three Types Of Quantum Attack

The paper classifies quantum attacks on cryptocurrencies into three categories, based on how fast the attacker needs to be: on-spend, at-rest and on-setup attacks.

On-spend attacks target transactions in transit. When someone broadcasts a bitcoin transaction, their public key becomes visible in the mempool. An attacker intercepts it, derives the private key, and broadcasts a fraudulent replacement before the original confirms. Bitcoin’s average block time is 10 minutes. Google’s paper estimates a superconducting quantum computer could complete the cracking in about nine minutes, using a technique where the machine precomputes half the algorithm and waits in a "primed" state until a target appears. With 11 primed machines running in parallel, the speedup reaches 6.5 times, bringing it well under the block time.
